This is a basic particle that introduces a contrasting idea, like saying 'but' or 'however' in English

steps.m: and yet/but still (contrasts with previous statement)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this contrasting idea with what came before it. This shows that even though the previous statement is true, something different or opposite follows.

steps.m: to curse or remove from mercy

steps.n: Connected the letters with fatha vowels to show this happened in the past

steps.m: indeed he cursed (emphatic, subject of Allah)
steps.n: Adding shadda makes the meaning stronger and more emphatic
Added هُمُ to show who is being cursed
Changed the word to refer specifically to Allah by adding ال and merging letters. The ل gets a shadda because it combines with the ل from ال.

Added final vowel mark to show this is the subject doing the action. The hamza at the start becomes a special connecting hamza (ٱ). This word is the subject (doer) of the verb لَّعَنَ (cursed) in this ayah.
Changed from action (to reject) into thing/concept (rejection) by adding vowel marks
Added kasra to connect with following pronoun
Added هِمْ to show it belongs to them
Added بِ at beginning to show reason or cause
This is a basic negative word that makes things negative, like adding 'not' in English
Added fatha mark to show correct pronunciation
Added فَ at the beginning to show this is a result of what came before. This word negates the verb يُؤْمِنُونَ (they believe) that comes after it.
Adding vowels to make it a past tense verb about feeling safe
Added an alif at the start to mean 'causing/making someone do the action' - changed from just feeling safe to actively putting trust in something/someone
Changed to present tense by adding يُ at start and changing vowels
Added plural ending to show multiple people believing

steps.m: except (what follows from what came before)
steps.n: This is a particle that excludes what comes after it from what came before. In this ayah, it excludes 'قَلِيلًا' (a few) from the previous statement about not believing.
The ي makes it into an adjective describing something that has the quality of being small in amount

steps.m: a little/few (describes how something is done in this ayah)
steps.n: Added ًا at the end to show this word describes how something is done

steps.m: to point to or indicate something

steps.n: Root letters combined with fatha and shadda to make a word that asks 'which one?'
Added calling particle يا with long vowel to get attention
Added هَا to make it a complete attention-calling phrase
This is a special word used to describe or point to someone or something. We start with its simplest form.
Added ين at the end to make it plural (many) instead of singular (one)
Added ال at the beginning to point to specific people or things
Changed the spelling of ال to ٱلَّ and added emphasis (shadda) on the ل. This is just a spelling change that doesn't affect the meaning.
Changed vowels to show this is happening to someone rather than someone doing it
The ت with damma before ي changes to و because of weak letter rules
Added وا at the end to show this happened to multiple people. This word describes ٱلَّذِينَ (those who) in this ayah.
Connected the root letters with basic past tense vowels
Changes from a verb (wrote) to a noun (book/writing)

steps.m: the book (object of أُوتُوا - were given)
steps.n: Added ال to make it specific and َ at the end to show it's being acted upon. This word is what was given to the people mentioned in أُوتُوا (were given)
Adding alif makes it mean 'to actively put trust in something'
Changed from past tense 'he believed' to command form 'you believe!' by changing middle vowel

steps.m: all of you believe! (command to a group)
steps.n: Added وا to make it a command to multiple people
This is a basic particle that points to something general or unknown

steps.m: in what/in that which (connects to نَزَّلْنَا - we sent down)
steps.n: Added بِ at the beginning to show this connects to something else. Here it connects to نَزَّلْنَا (we sent down) to show what was believed in.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
The doubled middle letter shows this happened multiple times or with extra force
Changed ending to make it ready for adding نا (meaning 'we')
Added نا to show 'we' are doing the action

steps.m: to speak truth or verify something

steps.n: Adding the double letter (ّ) in the middle makes the meaning stronger - changing it from just 'being truthful' to 'actively confirming or verifying something'. The vowels help us pronounce it correctly.
Adding مُ at the start and changing the middle vowel to ِ turns this from an action into a person or thing that does the action (like changing 'teach' to 'teacher' in English)
Adding ًا at the end shows this word is describing how or why something is being done. Here it's describing what was revealed (نَزَّلْنَا) - saying it confirms what came before.
Starting with the basic preposition that shows purpose or direction
Adding emphasis with shadda shows importance or certainty

steps.m: surely for what (referring to مُصَدِّقًا - confirming)
steps.n: Adding مَا makes it refer to something specific - in this case, it's referring to what was sent down as confirmation
This is a preposition meaning 'with' - it doesn't come from a root but is a basic word itself
Added كُم at the end to show this means 'with you all' instead of just 'with'
Basic preposition showing something comes from or originates from something else
Added emphasis by doubling the م letter. This emphasis shows up because the word before it ends with a ل sound (لِّمَا), which causes this strengthening in pronunciation.
Added fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter to make it a noun meaning 'before'

steps.m: before (of أَن نَّطْمِسَ - that we should obliterate)
steps.n: The kasra at the end shows this word is connected to what comes after it (أَن نَّطْمِسَ - that we should obliterate)

steps.m: that (connects to نَّطْمِسَ - we will obliterate)
steps.n: Basic connecting word that introduces what will happen - in this case, it connects to the next word نَّطْمِسَ (we will obliterate) to explain what will happen

steps.m: to wipe out completely, erase all traces of

steps.n: Added vowels to connect the root letters and make it a past tense verb
Added ن at start to show 'we' are doing it, changed vowels to show it's happening now/future
Added shadda on ن to make the meaning stronger, showing this is a serious warning
These are the basic letters that give us the idea of facing or turning toward something
Changed the verb into a noun meaning the physical face
Makes the singular 'face' into multiple 'faces'

steps.m: faces (as the object being acted upon by نَطْمِسَ - we obliterate)
steps.n: Added tanween fatha to show this word is the object being acted upon by the verb نَطْمِسَ (we obliterate)

steps.m: to send back or return something to where it was

steps.n: Adding the vowel marks shows this is a complete past tense verb
Added نَ at start to show 'we' are doing the action, and changed vowels to show present tense
Added فَ to show this happens after the previous action (after we obliterate faces)

steps.m: and then we will turn it back (the faces mentioned in نَّطْمِسَ وُجُوهًا)
steps.n: Added هَا to show what is being returned - referring back to the faces (وُجُوهًا) that were mentioned in the phrase 'we will obliterate faces' (نَّطْمِسَ وُجُوهًا)
Basic preposition showing position or direction
Added vowel marks to show correct pronunciation
Added alif khanjariyah (small alif) to show long vowel sound
Added vertical fatḥa (ٓ) to show connection to the next word أَدْبَارِهَآ (their backs)

steps.m: to turn back or follow behind

steps.n: Root letters د-ب-ر got short 'a' vowels (فتحة) to make the noun meaning 'back/behind'
Changed to plural by adding أ at start and ا after ب to make 'backs'
Added kasra at the end to show this word is being possessed by something
Added هَآ ('their') to show these are their backs/rear parts being referred to
This is a connecting word that joins two possible outcomes in this ayah - either 'We will deface faces' or 'We will curse them'. It's a basic word that doesn't come from a root.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Added ن at beginning to show 'we' are doing the action, and changed vowels to show present tense
Added هُمْ at the end to show who receives the action (them)
This is a comparison particle that shows something is similar to something else
Adding ما makes the comparison more specific and connects it to what follows. In this ayah, it's comparing a future curse to a past one mentioned right after (we cursed the Sabbath-breakers).
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb
Added sukun to prepare for adding 'we' ending
Added emphasis by doubling the ن with shadda
Added نا to show 'we' did the action
Connected the root letters with fatha to make a past tense verb
Changed pattern to show someone who does the action
Changed to broken plural form. In this ayah, these companions are the object of the verb 'we cursed' (لَعَنَّآ)
Changes from verb 'to rest' into noun meaning 'day of rest'
Adding definite article to make the noun definite

steps.m: of the Sabbath (possessed by or related to something)
steps.n: The kasra at the end shows this word is connected to something after it, like saying "of the Sabbath"
Added fatha marks and connected letters. The middle و changes to ا because it's a weak letter in the past tense.
Added و at the beginning to connect this word with what comes before it. This word connects to أَمْرُ (command) in the ayah.
Changed from verb 'to command' into noun 'a command' by adding specific vowel pattern
Added damma at the end to show this is the subject of كَانَ (was) in this ayah
Root meaning 'worship' became a noun meaning 'one who gets worshipped'
Added ال to make it specific and changed the first letter, making it a unique name for The One True God
Added emphasis by doubling the ل
Changed first letter to ٱ and added ِ at the end to show possession
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a basic past tense verb
Changed the word to show what receives the action rather than what does the action

steps.m: accomplished/done (as the object being described in أَمْرُ ٱللَّهِ)
steps.n: Added tanween (double damma + alif) to show this is the object receiving the action in the sentence
Added kasra (ِ) under the first letter to show this is the emphatic particle إِنَّ, not the conditional particle إِنْ

steps.m: indeed/truly (emphasizing ٱللَّهَ لَا يَغْفِرُ - Allah does not forgive)
steps.n: Added shadda (ّ) to strengthen the emphasis. This particle introduces and emphasizes the important statement that follows about what Allah does and doesn't forgive.
Added ال and merged letters to create the unique name for The One God. The first letter changes to ا and gets a special mark (waslah) showing connection in speech.

steps.m: Allah (as the object of يُشْرِكْ - associates partners with)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show Allah is the object being acted upon in this sentence - the one with whom partners are being associated. The alif at the start gets a special mark (waslah) showing it connects in speech.
This is used to say 'no' or to make a verb negative
Added fatha mark to show the correct pronunciation
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb

steps.m: he forgives (in response to لا meaning 'does not')
steps.n: Changed to present tense by: 1) adding يَ at start to show 'he' does it now, 2) adding sukun (ْ) on first root letter, 3) adding kasra (ِ) on second root letter, and 4) adding damma (ُ) at end to show this is the doer
This is a connecting word that joins the main verb يَغْفِرُ (He forgives) with what follows يُشْرَكَ (partners being associated). It shows what Allah does not forgive.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb

steps.m: to be associated as partners (with اللَّهِ)
steps.n: Changed to passive voice to show this is about partners being associated with Allah, rather than someone actively associating partners. This appears in a phrase about what Allah does not allow.
Basic preposition showing how something is done or connected

steps.m: with him/it (referring to يُشْرَكَ - being associated)
steps.n: Added pronoun هِ to show who/what is being referred to. This connects back to the act of associating partners mentioned in يُشْرَكَ.
Added small alif (ۦ) after هِ as a pronunciation guide to show the vowel is elongated slightly
This is the basic meaning that all words from this root share
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make it a past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start, changing vowels, and adding ُ at end

steps.m: and he forgives (connected to لَا يَغْفِرُ - does not forgive)
steps.n: Added و to connect this phrase to the previous statement about what Allah does not forgive
This is a relative pronoun that refers to unspecified things. In this ayah, it's the object of يَغْفِرُ (He forgives), showing what Allah forgives.
This describes something being below or less than something else

steps.m: other than (describes what Allah won't forgive)
steps.n: The damma (ُ) and fatha (َ) marks complete the word. In this ayah, it describes what Allah won't forgive - anything 'other than' associating partners with Him.
This is the basic form used to point to something nearby
The alif is written with a special mark (ٰ) to show we're pointing to something farther away
Added ل with kasra (ِ) which helps connect the word parts

steps.m: that (pointing to something far) (refers to what was just mentioned about Allah not forgiving shirk)
steps.n: Added ك with fatha (َ) at the end to show we're addressing a single male or speaking generally. In this ayah, it points back to what was just mentioned about Allah not forgiving shirk (associating partners with Him).
This combines 'for' (لِ) with 'whoever' (مَن) to mean 'for whoever'
This is about wanting or wishing for something
The middle letter ي changes to ا due to being a weak letter
Added يَ at beginning to show present tense and ُ at end to show he is doing the action
Added long ا to show the extended nature of willing/wishing
This is a conditional/interrogative particle that refers to people (like 'whoever' or 'who' in English)
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this with the previous statement. This word is the subject (doer) of the verb يُشْرِكْ (associates partners) in this ayah.
This is the basic idea of joining or sharing something with others
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a basic past tense verb
Changed from past to present tense (starts with يُ). The ending has no vowel mark (sukun) because it follows the word مَن in the ayah. Shows action of joining others with Allah.
This is the base word - Allah's proper name. It's unique and doesn't come from a root.
The alif at the beginning changes to a different form (ٱ) when connecting with other words. This doesn't change the meaning.
Added بِ at the beginning to show this is being done by or with Allah's power/permission

steps.m: by/with Allah (connected to يُشْرِكْ - associates partners)
steps.n: The ِ at the end shows this word is connected to the verb يُشْرِكْ (associates partners). It means someone is trying to associate partners with Allah.
This is a basic particle that shows something definitely happened
Added فَ at the beginning to show this happens as a result of the previous action (يُشْرِكْ - associates partners with)
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the letters
Added ٱ at start and ت in middle to show he made up something false on purpose
Added the alif maksura (ىٰ) with a small alif above it for pronunciation

steps.m: to commit a wrong or harmful act
steps.n: This is about doing something wrong or harmful
Changes from action to noun - now means the wrong act itself instead of doing it
Added tanween to show this is the object being fabricated in the phrase 'has fabricated a sin'

steps.m: to be mighty or great in size/importance
steps.n: This is about something being big or important
Added vowels to make it a past tense verb showing something becoming great
Changes from a verb (becoming great) to an adjective (being great)
Adds tanween fatHa to show this word is describing the sin (إِثْمًا) mentioned in the ayah
This particle turns a statement into a question that expects the answer 'yes'
Adding ل adds emphasis to show we're sure about what we're asking
Adding م makes the verb negative. Together with أَلَ it asks 'Haven't you?' expecting the answer 'Yes, I have'

steps.m: to see or observe with one's eyes or mind

steps.n: Changed from basic root to past tense 'he saw' by adding fathas and changing ي to ى at the end
Added تَ at beginning to show 'you' and removed أ because it comes between two vowels

steps.m: you see (in أَلَمْ تَرَ - 'do you not see?')
steps.n: The ى at the end is dropped because لَمْ makes verbs shorter - like cutting off the last letter
This is a basic preposition showing direction or destination. Since it's a particle, we start with its basic form rather than a root.
Added the basic vowel marks to show how the word is pronounced

steps.m: to, toward (pointing to الَّذِينَ - those who)
steps.n: Added the final alif maqsurah (ى) which completes the preposition. In this ayah, it connects to 'those who' (الَّذِينَ) that comes after it.
This is a demonstrative relative pronoun formed by adding the definite article ال and the masculine plural suffix ين with fatha

steps.m: to purify or make something grow better

steps.n: The doubled middle letter shows doing something thoroughly or with extra effort
Adding يُ at the start shows someone is doing this action now or in the future
Adding ونَ at the end shows that a group of people are doing this action
Basic noun form meaning 'a soul/self' from root letters meaning 'breath/spirit'
Changed from singular 'self' to plural 'selves' by adding أ at start and changing vowels - like going from 'cat' to 'cats' in English

steps.m: their selves (referring to those who claim to purify themselves)

steps.n: This is a basic particle that introduces a correction or contrast
Added vowel marks to show how the word is pronounced. This word contrasts with the previous statement about people claiming their own purity (يُزَكُّونَ), introducing Allah's role instead.
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'a deity' or 'something worshipped'
Added the U mark at the end to show this is the subject/doer

steps.m: to make something pure and good

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a past tense verb
Added shadda to show stronger action, and the weak letter و changed to ى at the end
Added يُ at the beginning to show present tense, and changed the middle vowel to kasra
Basic form of this word that refers to people in general (subject of يَشَآءُ - he wills)
Added fatha mark to show correct pronunciation. This word refers to the person or people that Allah wills to purify.
The root letters combine and get vowels to make 'he willed'. The middle letter ي turns into ا, which is common for this type of verb.
Changes from past to present tense by adding يَ at start and ُ at end
Adds long alif to show continuous/extended action of willing
This is the basic negation word that means 'not' in Arabic
Added و with fatha at the beginning to connect this word to what came before it
Connected the root letters with fatha to make basic past tense verb
Changed vowels to show this happened to someone instead of someone doing it
Added يُ at start to show this happens in present/future. Added sukun to make pronunciation easier.
Added ون at the end to show this happens to many people
Added ي between second and third root letters to show something that has been thoroughly twisted

steps.m: even a tiny thread (object of يُظْلَمُونَ)
steps.n: Added ًا to show this is what they won't be wronged by even the amount of, connected to يُظْلَمُونَ (they will not be wronged)
Connected the root letters with fathas to make the basic past tense verb
Changed the vowels to make it a command form, but it still needs one more change to be complete

steps.m: look! (command to look at كَيْفَ يَفْتَرُونَ - how they invent)
steps.n: Added ٱ at the beginning to complete the command form. This word is connected to what follows in the ayah - it's telling us to look at how they invent lies.
This is a question word that always asks about the way something happens
Adding vowel marks to make the word pronounceable
Adding the final vowel mark to complete the word
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to form past tense verb
Changed from past to present tense by adding ي at start
Added ت to make Form VIII, which adds intensity to the meaning

steps.m: they make up (something false) (subject of كَيْفَ)
steps.n: Changed from 'he fabricates' to 'they fabricate' - the word follows كَيْفَ (how) to show how they make up lies against Allah

Basic preposition that shows something is on top of, against, or directed towards something else. In this ayah, it connects to the word ٱللَّهِ (Allah) to show they are making up lies upon/against Allah.
Added ال to make it specific and unique, with special spelling for Allah's name where the أ is replaced with ل
The word 'Allah' gets a kasra (i) sound at the end because it's being acted upon - someone is lying about Allah. Also has a stronger L sound (shadda) and a special A at the start.
Adding vowels turns the root into a complete past tense verb meaning 'he lied'
Changed from the action 'to lie' into 'the lie' by adding ال and changing the word form

steps.m: the lie/falsehood (object of يَفْتَرُونَ - they invent)
steps.n: The fatha at the end shows this word is receiving the action of 'inventing' in the phrase 'they invent the lie'
Added fathas to make it past tense. The ى at the end is because this is a special type of word that ends in ي

steps.m: and he/it was sufficient (connected to يَفْتَرُونَ)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this with the previous action of them inventing lies. Also added the small alif (ٰ) which helps with pronunciation but doesn't change the meaning.
Added kasra to show this is a preposition meaning 'with' or 'by'

steps.m: with it/by it (referring to ٱلْكَذِبَ - the lie)
steps.n: Added the pronoun 'هِ' to show what is being referred to. Here it refers back to 'the lie' mentioned earlier in the verse.

steps.m: with it/by it (referring to ٱلْكَذِبَ - the lie)
steps.n: Added a small alif (ۦ) and elongation mark (ٓ) to help with pronunciation. This doesn't change the meaning.

steps.m: to do something harmful or wrong

steps.n: Changed the أ to إِ and added vowel marks to make it a noun meaning 'sin'
Added ًا to show this is what they are inventing/fabricating in the verse
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Added مُ at start to make it mean 'the one who' and changed inside vowels to show it's describing something that makes things clear
Added shadda to emphasize the meaning
Added tanween alif to show this word is describing how something is done. In this ayah, it's describing how the sin (إِثْمًا) is clearly apparent.
This is a basic negative word used to talk about things that didn't happen in the past
Adding أَ at the beginning turns this into a question that expects the answer 'yes'. It's like in English when we say 'Didn't you see that?' - we expect the person to say 'Yes, I did see that.'
Changes from base 'to see' into 'he sees' in present tense
Changed يَ to تَ to show 'you' instead of 'he'
The ى at the end is dropped because أَلَمْ (meaning 'did you not') comes before it. This is a common pattern in Arabic questions.
This is a basic preposition that shows direction. In this ayah, it connects 'look' (تَرَ) with 'those who' (ٱلَّذِينَ), meaning 'look to/at those who'.
Starting with the basic demonstrative ذا (this), we change it to the relative pronoun form by adding kasra and changing the ending
Adding ال at the beginning with a shadda makes it more specific, like saying 'the one who' instead of just 'who'
Adding ين at the end makes it plural, changing from 'the one who' to 'those who'
Changed to passive voice to show receiving rather than giving
The letter ت changes to و because it comes after ُ (damma)
Added وا to show multiple people were given something. These people are the ones who 'believe' (يُؤْمِنُونَ) in the verse.

steps.m: to establish, set up, or assign a portion
a portion, share or allotted part
steps.n: Changes from verb (to assign) to noun (a share/portion)

steps.m: a portion/share (object of أُوتُوا - they were given)
steps.n: Adding ًا shows this word is receiving the action of being given in the phrase 'they were given a portion'
Basic preposition showing something comes from or is compared with something else
Added emphasis (shadda) on the م because it comes after the word أَهْدَىٰ. This is a pronunciation helper and doesn't change the meaning.
Connected the root letters with basic past tense vowels
This pattern is often used for things that result from an action - a book is the result of writing. The word is in the genitive case because it comes after مِنَ (from/of).
The ِ ending shows this word comes after مِنَ (from/of) in the ayah
Makes the noun definite by adding the definite article
Changed alif to alif maqsurah - this is just a spelling variation with no meaning change
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Changes from past tense (he believed) to present tense (he believes). The root meaning of trust/belief stays the same.
Added plural ending for 'they'
Root letters get vowels (kasra-sukun) to make a noun meaning 'false god/idol'
Adding ال makes it refer to a specific false deity or object of worship

steps.m: in the false object of worship (connected to يُؤْمِنُونَ)
steps.n: Added بِ at start to show belief 'in' something, and kasra at end because it follows the preposition. This word is what they believe in, connected to يُؤْمِنُونَ (they believe).

steps.m: to go beyond proper limits, to rebel against what's right

steps.n: Changed to special noun pattern with وت ending that shows something that has gone to an extreme in rebellion
Adding definite article makes the noun definite

steps.m: of/in the false deity (object of يُؤْمِنُونَ - they believe in)
steps.n: The word gets a kasra (small diagonal line) at the end because it comes after the word 'in' (ب) and shows what they believe in

steps.m: and of/in the false deity (object of يُؤْمِنُونَ - they believe in)
steps.n: Adding conjunction prefix وَ meaning 'and'
Adds يَ at start to make it 'he says' in present tense
Adds conjunction و meaning 'and' to connect this action with what came before
Changes from singular (he) to plural (they) by adding ون, showing a group is doing the action
This is a special word (relative pronoun) that refers to people or things we're talking about. It's like saying 'the ones who' or 'those who' in English.

Added لِ at the beginning to show 'for/to'. When لِ is added to a word starting with ال, the ا disappears and the ل gets a shadda (double mark). This word connects to كَفَرُوا۟ (disbelieved) later in the ayah.
Added fathas to connect the root letters and make it past tense singular
Changed the verb from singular (he) to plural (they) by adding وا. This word is one of the subjects of يَقُولُونَ (they say) in this ayah.
The basic pointing word 'ha' gets written with an alif above it to show it's stretched when speaking
Added a small alif above to show the sound is stretched even more
Added ؤُ to show we're pointing to multiple things, not just one thing
Added لَآ to make it specifically point to people or things (not just a general direction)
Added ءِ at the end to complete the word's proper pronunciation

steps.m: to guide or show the way

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha vowels. The final ي changes to ى because it's at the end of the word.

steps.m: he caused to be guided/he made guide
steps.n: Adding أ at start makes it mean 'to cause someone to do the action'

steps.m: he caused to be guided/he made guide
steps.n: Adding alif maqsurah (ى with small alif) at end shows this is past tense
Basic preposition that shows where something comes from or compares things
Added fatha at the end to connect smoothly with the next word (ٱلْكِتٰبِ). In this ayah, it's used to show that some people received a portion from the Book.
This is a frozen form relative pronoun used to describe a group of people. It combines the definite article ٱلْ with the relative pronoun ذِي and the plural suffix نَ
Adding alif makes it mean 'to believe' instead of just 'to feel safe'
Added وا at the end to show many people did this action

steps.m: to make a way or path

steps.n: Adding ي and vowels changes it from a verb into a noun meaning 'path'
Added an alif at the end to show this is the thing being compared - like saying 'a better path' instead of just 'path'
This is the base form of the demonstrative pronoun used to point to things or people
The alif with line on top (ٰ) shows that what we're pointing to is far away

steps.m: those (pointing to people far away) (subject of لَعَنَهُمُ)
steps.n: Added ئِكَ at the end to show we're specifically pointing to people. This word is the subject of لَعَنَهُمُ (cursed them) in this ayah.
Added ال to make it definite and doubled the ذ letter to connect them smoothly. This word connects parts of sentences like 'who' in English.
Added ين at the end to show we're talking about multiple people. This word is connected to لَعَنَهُمُ (cursed them) in this ayah.

steps.m: to curse or reject from mercy

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to show this is Allah doing the action in the past
Added هُمُ at the end to show who received the curse - it refers back to أُو۟لٰٓئِكَ (those people) mentioned earlier
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'a deity' by adding specific vowel patterns
This is the unique name for The One True God in Islam. The ال makes it definite (like 'the'), the shadda emphasizes the ل, and the ُ shows this is the subject doing the action in this sentence.
Added fatha to help with pronunciation
Added و at the beginning to connect this word to the previous statement. This word is connected to يَلْعَنِ (curses) in the ayah, showing who is being cursed.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb
Changed from past to present tense by adding يَ at start and putting a sukun (little circle) on ل
The word gets a kasra (i) ending because it comes right before the word Allah (اللَّهُ), which is doing the cursing

steps.m: a deity, god, or object of worship
steps.n: Changed the root into a noun meaning 'a deity' by adding specific vowel patterns
Added the u-vowel at the end to show this is the subject
This is a negative particle that makes verbs show they won't happen in the future
Added فَ at the beginning to show this is a result or consequence. This word negates the verb يَجِدَ (find) that comes after it.
Added vowels to connect the root letters and make a past tense verb
Changed to present tense with 'you' by adding تَ at start and changing middle vowel to kasra. The word comes after فَلَن which means this will never happen in the future.
Added fatha to show this is a preposition meaning 'for' or 'to'
Added ه with damma to show this means 'him'
Added the small و mark (ۥ) which is just a pronunciation helper and doesn't change the meaning
Connected the root letters with fatha to make a basic past tense verb
Adding ي changes it from a verb (helped) to a noun meaning someone who helps a lot
Added tanween alif to show this is the object of the verb تَجِدَ (you will find) in this ayah

steps.m: or (connecting two equal options in a question)
steps.n: This is a connecting particle that joins two equal options in a question. In this ayah, it connects to a previous statement, asking if they have a share in authority.
Added fatha to help with pronunciation
Added هُمْ to show who the preposition is directed to. This word connects to نَصِيبٌ (a share/portion) to show who has the share.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Changed from verb 'establish' to noun meaning 'portion' by adding ي and kasra
Added two dots on top (tanween) to show this is 'what they have' - like saying 'a share' instead of just 'share'
Basic preposition showing something comes from or belongs to something else
The letter م has a shaddah (double mark) to blend smoothly with the word after it
Added fatha at the end to connect this word to the next word الْمُلْكِ (the dominion/kingdom), showing what something is from

steps.m: to rule or have authority over something

steps.n: Changed the root letters into a noun by adding ُ (u-sound) on first letter and ْ (no vowel) on middle letter to show the thing that results from ruling
Added ٱلْ (al-) at the beginning to point to a specific kingdom or authority

steps.m: of the kingdom/authority (possessed by someone or following مِنَ)
steps.n: Added ِ (i-sound) at the end because it comes after مِنَ (from/of) in this ayah
This is the base word that refers to a specific time or situation
Adding tanween alif (ًا) changes the meaning from just 'when' to 'in that case'

steps.m: then in that case (connects to لَّا يُؤْتُونَ)
steps.n: Adding فَ at the beginning shows this is a result of what came before. In this ayah, it connects to لَّا يُؤْتُونَ (they do not give) to show the consequence.
Basic negative particle that negates what comes after it
The shadda (ّ) adds emphasis to make the negation stronger. This word is negating يُؤْتُونَ (they give) in this ayah.
Changes from basic root to 'he' form in present tense - adds يَ at start
Added sukun (ْ) on hamza to show it's not connected to next letter. This is a spelling/pronunciation change only.
Added plural ending ون to show multiple people doing the action. This word is the subject of لَّا (not) in this ayah.

steps.m: to be social, familiar with others
people (those who are social/familiar with each other)
steps.n: The root meaning 'to be social' becomes a word for 'people' by dropping the first letter and changing the vowels. Like how we get 'people' from 'being social with each other'
Adding the shadda (doubling mark) makes the word stronger, like saying 'the true/real people'
Added ال to specify these particular people

steps.m: the people (as the object being acted upon in يُؤْتُونَ - they give)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show these people are receiving the action of giving in the phrase 'they do not give the people'

steps.m: to make a small mark or hole by pecking

steps.n: Added vowels to connect the letters into a basic past-tense verb
Changes from verb (to mark) into noun (a mark/speck)

steps.m: even a speck/dot (object of يُؤْتُونَ - they give)
steps.n: Added tanween to show this is the object being acted upon. In this ayah, it means they won't give people even the tiniest amount (like a speck on a date seed).
This is a special kind of 'or' that connects to a previous question, asking which of two options is true. In this ayah, it connects to an implied earlier question.

steps.m: to envy or be jealous of someone's blessings

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form past tense
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels
Added ون to show multiple people doing the action. In this ayah, they are envying النَّاسَ (the people)
Root letters connected with short 'a' vowels (fatha) to make a simple noun meaning 'closeness'

steps.m: people (those who are close to each other)
steps.n: Changed from a verb about being close to a plural noun meaning 'people' by adding ا after ن
Added shadda to emphasize the meaning, showing these are definitely 'the people'
Adding 'ال' makes it specific - we're talking about particular people, not just any people
The fatha at the end shows this word is being acted upon in the sentence
This is the base form meaning something high or elevated
Adding ى at the end turns this into a preposition showing something is on top of or over something else
The small alif (ٰ) is added to show this is a special form of ى that doesn't change at the end of words
The alif is lengthened to مَآ from مَا to indicate the relative/interrogative meaning
Changed the first letter to hamza with fatha and added long alif to make it causative - making someone/something come/bring
Added ى at the end to complete the past tense form of the verb
Added هُمُ at the end to show who received the action. This word is what Allah gave to the people who are being envied in this verse.
The word has هم (them) at the end to show who received the giving
Changed the concept of worship into a noun meaning 'a deity'
Added the u-mark at the end to show it's the subject

steps.m: from (connects فَضْلِ to what comes before it)
steps.n: This is a preposition that shows where something comes from or its source. In this ayah, it connects Allah's bounty (فَضْلِ) to what was mentioned before.

steps.m: to give extra, to be generous

steps.n: Root meaning 'extra' becomes a noun meaning 'bounty' by adding marks (fatha-sukun-nothing)
Added kasra at the end because it comes after the word مِن (from)
Added هِ at the end to show it belongs to Him (Allah)
Added vowel marks to show correct pronunciation. قَدْ is a particle that adds emphasis and certainty to what follows.
Added فَ at the beginning to connect this idea with what follows. This shows that one thing leads to another - in this case, connecting to the verb ءَاتَيْنَآ (We gave).
Connected root letters with fatha marks. The ي changes to ى at the end because it's a weak letter.
Adding hamza and long alif at beginning makes the action affect others directly
The ى changes back to يْ before adding a suffix
Adding نَآ at the end shows 'we' did the action
The alif with madd (آ) is added to make the word a noun meaning 'family/followers'
This is a special nominal form where the weak middle letter is lengthened to ا
This is a foreign proper name that was incorporated into Arabic. It does not follow standard Arabic morphological patterns and has no Arabic root.
Added vowel marks and elongated alif (ٰ) for proper pronunciation. The fatha at the end shows this is the object (receiver) of the verb 'we gave' (ءَاتَيْنَآ) in this ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Changed the vowel pattern and added alif to make it into a noun for the result of writing
Added the definite article ال to specify this particular book
Added fatha at the end because it's the object being given in the sentence. The alif is written as a long alif (ٰ) in this style.
Root meaning 'wise' becomes the noun 'wisdom' by adding the vowels i and sukoon
Added ة to change from general wisdom to a specific piece of wisdom or wise saying
Added ال to make it refer to specific wisdom being discussed
Added و to connect with previous word and َ at end to show this is the object receiving the action of giving (ءَاتَيْنَآ means 'We gave')
Changed the root to make it mean 'causing something to happen' instead of just happening
Changed the final ى to ي to prepare for attachments
Added نا at the end to show 'we' did the action
Added هُم at the end to show who received the action
Added و at the beginning to connect this with the previous statement
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a past tense verb
Changed vowels to make a noun that means the result of ruling - a kingdom or power
Adding shadda to م makes the meaning stronger and more intense

steps.m: a mighty kingdom/power (as object of ءَاتَيْنٰهُم)
steps.n: Added tanween to show this is the object being given in the phrase 'We gave them'
Added ي between ظ and م with vowels to make it into a word that describes something as having this quality
Added ًا at the end to show this describes the kingdom (مُّلْكًا) mentioned in the ayah
This is a basic preposition showing source or part of a group. In this ayah, it's used to divide people into groups - 'among them are those who...'
The فَ shows this is a result or sequence from what was mentioned before

steps.m: so from/among them (referring to the people mentioned)
steps.n: Added هُم which means 'them', referring to the people being discussed. The ْ (sukun) is added to connect the words smoothly.
This is a special word that introduces a new person or group in a sentence. Here it refers to the people who turned away.
The shadda (ّ) on م and sukun (ْ) at the end help with pronunciation when connecting to the next word
Connected the letters together with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Changed the first letter to hamza and added alif after it. This makes the verb causative, changing 'feel safe' to 'trust/believe in something that makes you feel safe'
This tiny word connects things, showing how they relate to each other
Added هِ which means 'him/it' (masculine). This shows what is being believed in, connecting to the verb ءَامَنَ (believed).

steps.m: from/among (shows part of the group who believed in the previous phrase فَمِنْهُم مَّنْ ءَامَنَ بِهِۦ)
steps.n: Basic preposition showing source or part of a group. Here it's splitting people into different groups - some who believed and some who turned away.

steps.m: from/among (shows part of the group who believed in the previous phrase فَمِنْهُم مَّنْ ءَامَنَ بِهِۦ)
steps.n: Added sukun (ْ) on ن to show end of word part
The word 'and' (وَ) was added to the beginning of 'from/among' (مِنْ)

steps.m: and from them (referring to those who turned away, contrasting with those who believed)
steps.n: هُم means 'them' (for a group of males or mixed group). Here it refers to those who turned away from believing, contrasting with the previous group who did believe.
This is the basic form of the relative pronoun used to refer to people, like saying 'who' or 'whoever' in English
The shadda (ّ) adds emphasis to show this is specifically about those people who turned away. This word is connected to صَدَّ (turned away) in the ayah, showing who did the turning away.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make it a past tense verb

steps.m: he turned away forcefully (from عَنْهُ - from it/him)
steps.n: The doubled middle letter shows this was done with force or intensity. This word connects to عَنْهُ which shows what was turned away from.
This is a basic preposition showing movement or turning away from something. It's one of the most common prepositions in Arabic.

steps.m: away from him (referring to those who turned away from believing in the message)
steps.n: Added هُ (him) to show who is being turned away from. In this ayah, it refers to those who turned away from believing in the message.
Added fathas to make it past tense. The ى at the end is because this is a special type of word that ends in ي
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this with the previous part
Added small alif (ٰ) above the ى to show it's pronounced as a long 'aa' sound
This is the proper name for Hell in Islamic tradition. The shadda (ّ) shows emphasis on the letter ن.
The بِ at the start shows this is being used with something else. Here it's connected to the word كَفَىٰ (sufficient) to show what is sufficient.
Added ي between the last two root letters and vowel marks to create a noun meaning something that burns intensely

steps.m: as a blazing fire (object of كَفَىٰ)
steps.n: Added tanween alif (ًا) to show this is the object being acted upon. In this ayah, it completes the phrase 'Hell is sufficient as a blazing fire'
This is a particle that has no root. It emphasizes the truth of what follows and makes اللَّهَ (Allah) take the special ending َ. In this ayah, it emphasizes that Allah truly was mighty and wise.
This is a basic word used to connect descriptions to people or things, like saying 'who' or 'which' in English
Adding ال makes it point to something specific, like changing 'who' to 'the one who'

steps.m: those who (subject of كَفَرُوا۟ - they disbelieved)
steps.n: Adding ين makes it refer to many people instead of just one. This word connects to كَفَرُوا۟ (they disbelieved) that comes after it, showing who did the disbelieving.

steps.m: to reject or deny truth, to cover up or hide something

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make it a past tense verb. This shows one person (he) did the action in the past.
Added وا at the end to show many people (they) did the action. This word is connected to إِنَّ (indeed/verily) at the start of the sentence.

steps.m: to show, make clear, be evident

steps.n: Changes the basic idea of showing into a noun meaning 'a sign that shows something'
Adds a long alif at the start to emphasize the meaning
Makes the word plural to show many signs
Adds بِ to show these signs are being used for something, and adds ِ to connect it to the next word
Adds نَا to show these are Allah's signs being mentioned

steps.m: will (something that will definitely happen in the future)
steps.n: This is a word that shows something will definitely happen in the future, but not right away. It's stronger than just saying 'will' - it's like saying 'will definitely' or 'will surely'. In this ayah, it's connected to نُصْلِيهِمْ (We will burn them) to show this punishment will certainly happen.
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the root letters
Added نُ at start to show 'we' are doing the action, and changed vowels to show we're making something happen

steps.m: we will cause them to burn (referring to disbelievers mentioned in كَفَرُوا)
steps.n: Added هِمْ at the end to show who is receiving the action. This refers back to the disbelievers mentioned earlier in كَفَرُوا (those who disbelieved)
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a weak letter. This also makes the meaning specifically about fire rather than light.

steps.m: fire (object of نُصْلِيهِمْ - We will burn them in)
steps.n: Added tanween alif to show this is the object being acted upon - the fire that they will be burned in
The doubled ل (shadda) makes the meaning stronger, showing it means absolutely everything with no exceptions
The fatha at the end shows this word is being used as a direct object

steps.m: whenever (connects to نَضِجَتْ meaning 'became fully cooked')
steps.n: Adding ما changes this from just meaning 'all/every' to meaning 'whenever' - it introduces the timing of when something happens. In this verse, it tells us WHEN new skins will be given - whenever the previous ones are fully cooked.

steps.m: it became fully cooked (state of جُلُودُهُم/their skins)
steps.n: Connected letters with vowels to show this describes a process or state change. This word describes what happened to their skins (جُلُودُهُم) in the hellfire.
Added feminine marker ت because جُلُودُهُم (their skins) is feminine in Arabic
Added vowel marks (kasra and sukoon) to the root letters to make the word mean 'a single skin'
Changes from single skin (jild) to plural skins (julood) by changing the vowels inside the word

steps.m: their skins (subject of نَضِجَتْ - became fully cooked)
steps.n: Adds هُم to show possession (their) and ُ to show this is the subject doing the action in نَضِجَتْ (became fully cooked)
The doubled د shows this is done thoroughly or completely
Added نا to show 'we' did the action instead of just a command
Added هم to show who was being replaced
Added vowels to the root letters to make the basic word for 'one skin'
Changes from single skin (جَلْد) to many skins by adding 'u' sounds and و
Adds tanween alif to show this word is the object being replaced in the verse

steps.m: to change or be different from something

steps.n: Added fatha and sukun to make a noun meaning 'other than'. This word describes جُلُودًا (skins) in the ayah.
Added ها which refers back to the previous skins mentioned. The fatha on ر shows this word describes جُلُودًا (skins).
Connected the root letters and added vowels to make past tense
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels
Added وا to show multiple people doing the action

steps.m: so that they may taste (the punishment mentioned in نُصْلِيهِمْ)
steps.n: Added لِ to show this is happening for a purpose. This word connects to 'the punishment' mentioned earlier in the verse.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb

steps.m: punishment (what is being tasted in يَذُوقُوا)
steps.n: Adding an alif between the second and third root letters turns this into a noun meaning the result of the punishment. This word is what is being tasted in the verb يَذُوقُوا (they taste).

steps.m: the punishment (what is being tasted in يَذُوقُوا)
steps.n: Adding ال makes it refer to a specific punishment, and the َ (fatha) at the end shows this is what's being acted upon

steps.m: indeed/verily (emphasizes the truth of ٱللَّهَ كَانَ عَزِيزًا حَكِيمًا)
steps.n: Adding shadda (ّ) on the ن changes this from a basic word meaning 'if/that' into a stronger word that emphasizes the truth of what follows. Here it emphasizes that Allah truly is mighty and wise.
Added ال and merged letters to create the unique name for The One God. The hamza is dropped and the lam gets a shadda.

steps.m: Allah (as the object being described as عَزِيزًا حَكِيمًا - Mighty and Wise)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show Allah is being described. The alif at the start becomes a wasl alif (ٱ) because it connects with the previous word.
Added vowels to connect the letters. This is a theoretical form before the weak letter change.
The و (weak letter) changes to ا, which is a common change in hollow verbs
Adding ي in the middle shows this describes someone or something that has a lot of strength or power
Adding ًا shows this word is describing Allah in the phrase كَانَ عَزِيزًا (He was/is mighty)

steps.m: to judge wisely or make wise decisions

steps.n: Added ي between second and third root letters to show this describes someone who has wisdom as a strong quality
Added ًا (an) at the end to make the word 'wise' match with how it's used to describe Allah in this sentence
This is the basic form of the word that means 'who'
Added ال at the beginning and doubled the ذ to make it point to a specific person or thing
Added ين at the end to make it refer to many people instead of just one
Added و at the beginning to connect it with what came before. This word is the subject of the verb ءَامَنُوا۟ (they believed) in this ayah.
This is the basic concept of trust and safety
Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
Added alif after hamza to change meaning from general trust to specifically religious belief/faith

steps.m: they believed (connected to وَٱلَّذِينَ - those who)
steps.n: Added وا at the end to show many people did this action. This word describes 'those who' (وَٱلَّذِينَ) at the start of the verse.
Connected the root letters with vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Added وا at the end to show multiple people did the action
Added و at beginning to connect this action with the previous word ءَامَنُوا۟ (they believed), showing these people both believed and did good works
Connected the root letters with fatha to make a basic past-tense verb
Added alif after first letter and kasra after second letter to show someone who has this quality
Added ات to make it refer to multiple feminine things/people that are righteous
Added ال to make it specific/definite - now talking about specific righteous women
Changed ending to show this word receives the action of 'doing/performing' (عَمِلُوا۟)
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the past tense verb
Added hamza at beginning to make the verb causative - changing from doing the action to making someone else do it
Changed to present tense 'we' form by adding نُ at start and changing vowels
Added س at beginning to show this will happen in the future

steps.m: we will cause them to enter (referring to those who believe and do good deeds)
steps.n: Added هُمْ at end meaning 'them' - refers back to 'those who believe and do good deeds' mentioned earlier in the verse
Added ة to make it mean a single garden, since gardens are places that have covering trees and plants
Changed to feminine plural form to show multiple gardens. This word is what's being acted on in the sentence - Allah will enter the believers into gardens.
Added the tanwin kasra (ٍ) to show this is the object being acted upon in سَنُدْخِلُهُمْ (We will enter them into)
Added vowels to connect the root letters and show past tense
Changed to present tense feminine form. This word describes 'the rivers' (الْأَنْهٰرُ) in this ayah, showing how they are actively flowing.
This is a preposition showing where the rivers flow from - beneath these gardens. It connects the verb تَجْرِى (flow) with تَحْتِهَا (beneath them).
Adding vowel marks to make the basic noun meaning 'under'
Adding kasra at the end makes it work as a preposition
Added ها to show this is under the gardens (جَنَّات) mentioned earlier
Root meaning 'flow' becomes the name for a stream/river
The middle letter gets a sukoon mark to make it a common noun form
Changed from single river to plural rivers by adding 'a' at start and end
Added definite article ال
Added nominative case ending because this word is the subject of تَجْرِى (flows/flowing) in this ayah
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the letters
Changed the pattern to show who does the action

steps.m: those staying permanently (describes the state of those entering Paradise)
steps.n: Added ين to show it's talking about many people. The alif changed to a special long alif (ٰ). This word describes the state of the people being entered into Paradise.
This is a preposition showing location or containment. It's one of the basic Arabic prepositions that doesn't come from a root.
Added ها which refers back to the gardens (جَنَّاتٍ) mentioned earlier in the verse. This shows what is being referred to as the location.
The alif is elongated (آ instead of ا) because it comes at the end of a phrase in the Quran. This is a pronunciation helper that doesn't change the meaning.
Connected the root letters and added vowels to make a noun meaning endless time

steps.m: forever (describing how long they will stay in جَنَّاتٍ/gardens)
steps.n: Added ًا (an) at the end to make it work as a word that tells us 'how long' - forever/eternally

steps.m: for/to (connected to سَنُدْخِلُهُمْ - we will admit them)
steps.n: This is a preposition showing direction or purpose
Adding shadda makes the meaning stronger and more certain
Added هم which shows this applies to a group of men or mixed group
This is a common preposition that shows location or containment. In this ayah, it refers to being inside the gardens (جَنَّاتٍ) mentioned earlier.
Added ها to show we're talking about being inside the gardens (جَنَّاتٍ) mentioned earlier. The ها is feminine because جَنَّاتٍ (gardens) is feminine.
The alif is elongated to آ for emphasis when pausing at the end of this word. This is a common Quranic writing style that helps with proper recitation.
This root relates to things that come in pairs or matching sets
Added vowels to make it into a noun meaning one person of a pair
This is a broken plural form that changes the word's pattern to show multiple spouses. In this ayah, it refers to the pure spouses in Paradise.
Added the ٌ ending to show this word is the subject being described as pure (مُّطَهَّرَةٌ) in this ayah
This root relates to physical and spiritual cleanliness
Adding shadda makes the purification more thorough or complete
Adding مُ shows this describes something that receives the purification
When the letter before م has a sukoon (no vowel), the م gets doubled with a shadda to make it easier to say
Adding ة makes this feminine to match أَزْوٰجٌ (spouses) which is feminine in this context
Adding tanween (double dammah) makes the word indefinite, meaning 'a' purified one rather than 'the' purified one
This root relates to going inside or entering something
Adding the vowel marks makes this a complete past tense verb meaning 'he entered'
Changes from 'he entered' to 'we make enter' - like when we let someone into a house
Adding third person masculine plural object pronoun suffix هم to indicate 'them'

steps.m: and we cause them (males) to enter
steps.n: Added و meaning 'and' to connect with previous statement
This root relates to providing shade or covering
Adding the kasra changes it from a verb (to shade) into a noun (shade/shadow)

steps.m: a deep shade (that will be provided in Paradise)
steps.n: The word gets a tanween (double line) at the end to show it's 'a shade' instead of 'the shade', and shows it's being acted upon in the sentence
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create the basic past tense verb
Changed from a verb (he shaded) to a describing word meaning 'very shady' by adding ي in the middle

steps.m: deeply shading, providing complete shade (describing جَنَّاتٍ - gardens)
steps.n: This word describes how the gardens mentioned earlier provide complete shade. The ًا ending shows it's describing the gardens.
This is a special word that adds emphasis to what follows. Here it emphasizes that it is truly Allah who commands.
The shadda (ّ) on ن makes the emphasis even stronger, like saying 'most certainly' instead of just 'certainly'
Changed the root letters into a noun for the one who receives worship

steps.m: Allah (the one true God who is being described in this ayah)
steps.n: The word for 'deity' became the special name 'Allah' by adding Al- in a unique way and doubling the L sound. The -a ending shows Allah is being described here.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb
Adding يَ at start and changing vowels shows this is happening now. The sukun (ْ) on أ and damma (ُ) on م are part of the present tense pattern.

steps.m: he orders/is ordering you all (subject of إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ)
steps.n: Adding كُمْ shows who is being ordered. This word is what Allah (إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ) is doing in this ayah.
This is a connecting word that joins يَأْمُرُكُمْ (He commands you) with تُؤَدُّوا (to return/deliver), showing what Allah is commanding.

steps.m: to return or deliver something to its rightful owner

steps.n: Adding vowel marks to make the basic past tense verb form
Changed from 'he' to 'you' and made it present tense, with extra emphasis on the action

steps.m: you all deliver/return (command to return الْأَمٰنٰتِ/the trusts)
steps.n: Changed to plural command form. This word is connected to الْأَمٰنٰتِ (the trusts) which comes after it in the ayah, showing what should be delivered/returned.
Adding alif after the second letter changes this root from 'to trust' into the noun 'safety/security'
Added ة at the end to show one specific thing that is trusted to someone (like a responsibility or duty)

steps.m: multiple responsibilities or things entrusted to someone
steps.n: Regular feminine plural formed by adding ات to the singular form
Adding ال makes it specific/definite - now talking about particular trusts
Adding kasra at the end shows this word is affected by a preposition
This is a basic preposition that shows direction or destination. It connects the word أَمٰنٰتِ (trusts) to أَهْلِهَا (their people), showing the trusts should be given back to their rightful owners.
Added an alif that looks like ى but with a small mark above (called alif maqsurah). This is just a spelling variation and doesn't change the meaning.
Added a small mark (called a maddah) that shows the sound is stretched slightly when reading. This doesn't change the meaning.

steps.m: to belong, to be familiar with

steps.n: Connected the root letters and added vowel marks to make it a noun meaning people who belong together
Added kasra at the end to show this word is owned by or belongs to something
Added ها to show these are the people who belong to or own the trusts (الأمٰنٰتِ) mentioned in the ayah

steps.m: when (introduces the condition for حَكَمْتُم - you judge)
steps.n: This is a conditional word that introduces a time or situation. Here it introduces when people are in the position of judging between others.

steps.m: and when (connects to previous command about trusts)
steps.n: Adds 'and' to connect this new command about judging fairly with the previous command about returning trusts. Both are instructions about dealing justly with others.

steps.m: to make a decision or judgment

steps.n: Connected the root letters together with fatha marks to make it a past tense verb
Added sukun on م to prepare for adding the ending that shows who did the action
Added تُم at the end to show that multiple people being spoken to (you all) did the action. This verb is describing what these people did in judging between الناس (the people).

steps.m: to separate or be between things

steps.n: Adding vowels changes this from a root into a usable word meaning 'between'
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a basic past tense verb

steps.m: people (those who are close/familiar with each other)
steps.n: Changed from a verb meaning 'became familiar' to a plural noun meaning 'familiar ones/people' by rearranging letters
Adding ال makes it specific/definite - now means 'the' people rather than just any people
Adding kasra at the end shows this word is being used as the object of a preposition

This is a connecting word that introduces what Allah is commanding. It connects the verb يَأْمُرُكُمْ (commands you) to the action being commanded تُؤَدُّوا (return/fulfill). It's like saying 'Allah commands you THAT you should...'
Basic past tense verb form with fatha vowels
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels
Changed from 'he' to 'you' by replacing يَ with تَ at start
Added وا at the end to show multiple people are doing the action
Changed from an action (acting fairly) to a concept (justice)
Added 'the' to make it specific
Added 'with' at the start and a special ending to show it's being used with the verb تَحْكُمُوا (to judge)
Added kasra (ِ) under the alif to show how to pronounce it correctly
Added shadda (ّ) to make the emphasis stronger. This word emphasizes that Allah is the one being described in what follows.
Changed to the unique name for the One True God by adding ال and merging letters. The shadda shows emphasis.
Added fatha at the end because Allah is the object of إِنَّ (indeed/verily) in this ayah. Also changed the initial alif to a wasl form since it connects with the previous word.
Added vowels to make past tense verb showing excellence
Adding shadda makes it an exclamation of praise
Adding alif makes it a complete exclamation of praise

steps.m: to give good advice or remind about right and wrong

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense form
Changes from past to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing middle vowel to ِ. Shows ongoing advice/teaching.
Adds كُم to show the advice is being given to multiple people
This is a basic preposition that shows how something is done or connected
Added the pronoun 'it' to show what is being referred to
Added pronunciation helpers (ۦٓ) that don't change the meaning
This is a particle that emphasizes what comes after it. In this ayah, it emphasizes that Allah (ٱللَّهَ) truly has these qualities. The shadda (ّ) makes the emphasis even stronger.
Changed to the unique name for the One True God by adding ال and merging letters
Added fatha at the end because this word is the object of إِنَّ (indeed/verily) in this ayah. Also changed initial alif to wasl form since it comes after another word.
This is a basic three-letter root that means existence or being
Added vowels (fatha) to make it past tense 'he was'
Added alif after ك to make it past tense of 'to be'
This is a basic three-letter root related to hearing
Added vowels and the letter ي to show this describes someone who hears in a complete and perfect way. When used for Allah, it means He hears everything.

steps.m: All-Hearing (as a quality of Allah in كَانَ سَمِيعًۢا)
steps.n: Added ًا at the end because this word comes after كَانَ (was). When كَانَ describes something, it needs this ending. Here it's telling us that Allah was and always is All-Hearing.
Creates intensive form showing complete ability to see/perceive. In this ayah, it describes Allah's complete awareness.
Adds tanween fatHa to show this describes how Allah was/is in the phrase كَانَ سَمِيعًۢا بَصِيرًا (He was/is All-Hearing, All-Seeing)
Adding damma to show it's the subject
Adding ها changes it to a calling/addressing word
Adds يٰٓ at the beginning to call attention, with elongated alif to emphasize the call
This is the basic form meaning 'who' or 'which'. We add ي to make it work with singular things or people.
Adding ن at the end makes it talk about many people instead of just one.

steps.m: those who (referring to believers in this ayah)
steps.n: Adding ال at the beginning makes it specific, like saying 'the ones who' instead of just 'ones who'. Here it refers to the believers who are being addressed.

steps.m: those who (referring to believers in this ayah)
steps.n: The ل gets a shadda (double mark) and the ا changes to ٱ to help with pronunciation. This doesn't change the meaning.
Added vowels to connect the letters and make a past tense verb
Changed the word to Form IV pattern by adding alif after first letter, which changes meaning to 'cause to trust/feel secure' → 'believe/have faith'
Added plural marker وا with elongation (ـٓ) and small و (۟) to show this is a group of people
Connected the root letters with vowels to form basic past tense verb
Adding أ at start changes meaning to 'make someone do the action'

steps.m: you all obey! (plural command to اللَّهَ - Allah)
steps.n: Changed from past tense 'he obeyed' to a command 'obey!' by adding وا at the end to make it plural - telling multiple people to obey Allah
Changed to the unique name 'Allah' by adding ال and merging letters. The shadda shows emphasis.
Added fatha at the end to show Allah is being acted upon - He is the one being obeyed in this verse. Also changed alif to wasl alif since it comes after another word.
Added vowels to connect the root letters and make a basic past tense verb
The أ at the start shows 'I' and changes it to present tense. The middle letter changes from ا to ي in this pattern.
Added وا at the end to change from 'I obey' to 'you all obey!' (command form)
Added و meaning 'and' to connect this command with the previous one
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Changed from a verb (sent) to a noun (messenger) by adding و
Adding ال makes it refer to a specific messenger (in this case, Prophet Muhammad ﷺ)
The fatha at the end shows this word is receiving the action of the verb أَطِيعُوا۟ (obey)
This is a special word that doesn't come from a root. It's always used to show possession or belonging. The ُ shows it's the subject of a sentence, and the ِ shows it connects to what comes after it.

steps.m: and the possessors of (of الْأَمْرِ/the authority)
steps.n: Added وَ at the beginning to mean 'and'. The small circle (۟) above و is just a pronunciation mark. This word connects to الْأَمْرِ (the authority) that comes after it in the ayah.
Added vowel marks to create the noun form of the word
Adding ال makes it refer to a specific command/authority

steps.m: of the command/authority (possessed by أُو۟لِى - those in authority)
steps.n: The kasra shows this word belongs to or is connected to أُو۟لِى (those in authority) in the phrase أُو۟لِى ٱلْأَمْرِ (those in authority)
This is a preposition that doesn't come from a root. The kasra (ِ) helps connect it to the next part.

steps.m: from you all (those being commanded in أَطِيعُوا)
steps.n: Added كُمْ at the end to show this applies to multiple people being addressed. This connects to the earlier command أَطِيعُوا (obey), showing who is being told to obey.
This is a conditional particle that shows what follows is a condition. In this ayah, it introduces what should happen if Muslims disagree about something.

steps.m: then if (connects this condition to what came before)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to connect this condition with the previous command about obeying Allah and His Messenger. It shows this is what you should do next.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Adding alif shows interaction between two parties
Adding تَ at the beginning shows people doing the action to each other
Changed from 'they' to 'you all' in the past by adding تُمْ at the end
This is a basic preposition that shows location or topic. Here it connects to the word شَىْءٍ (thing) to show 'in something'.
The ي changes to ى at the end of words in Arabic writing. This is just a spelling change and doesn't affect the meaning.
Connected the letters with fatha on first letter and sukun on second letter to make a basic noun. This word comes after the preposition في (in/concerning) in this ayah.
Added two dots under (ٍ) at the end to show it belongs to something, since it comes after the word في (in/at/on)
Command to one person to 'return' - middle letter has a shadda (doubled)
Adding وا changes command from singular to plural
Adding هُ shows what is being returned

steps.m: so return it! (command to many people)
steps.n: Adding فَ shows this is a result or consequence

This is a basic preposition that shows direction or destination. In this ayah, it connects the command 'return it' (رُدُّوهُ) with 'Allah' (ٱللَّهِ), showing where/to whom something should be returned.
Added vowel marks to show exact pronunciation: kasra (ِ) under the إ and fatha (َ) above the ل
Changed from any deity to specifically Allah, the One True God, by adding ال and merging letters
Added kasra at the end to show this connects to the word إِلَى (to) before it. Also added emphasis (shadda) on ل and changed initial alif to wasl form since it comes after another word.
The و makes it mean someone who does this action as their main role
Added ال to show we're talking about a specific messenger, and doubled the ر letter because it comes after ال
Added و at the beginning to connect this word with the previous words in the ayah
Added kasra at the end because this word is connected to the word إِلَى (to) that came before it
This is a basic conditional particle that introduces an if-statement. In this ayah, it connects the condition 'if you believe' to what came before.
Added the kasra (ِ) mark under the إ to show how it's pronounced (in). This doesn't change the meaning, it just helps with pronunciation.
This root represents the basic concept of existing or being in a state
Added damma and sukun to make the basic command form. The weak letter و drops out in this form.
Added تُ at the end to make it mean 'I was' or 'you were' instead of 'be!'
Changed from 'I was' to 'you all were' by adding م at the end. This matches with تُؤْمِنُونَ (you all believe) later in the verse.

steps.m: to trust, believe in, feel secure with

steps.n: Adding long alif at start changes meaning to 'make someone do the action'
Changed to present tense by adding يُ at start and changing vowels
Changed from 'he' to 'you' by replacing يُ with تُ
Added ونَ to show multiple people are doing the action. This word connects to اللَّهِ (Allah) in the ayah, showing what they believe in.
Connected the root letters to form the basic word for deity/god
Added shadda to emphasize this is the one true God, not just any deity
Added بِ at the beginning to show this is being used to swear by or connect with Allah
Added the special 'hamzat wasl' (ٱ) and a kasra ending to connect with next word
Connected the root letters with vowel marks to make it a noun
Added kasra at the end to show this word is connected to what comes after it
Added ال to make it specific

steps.m: and of/in the day (connected to بِٱللَّهِ - belief in Allah)
steps.n: Added و to connect with previous phrase about believing in Allah. The word is part of the phrase 'believing in Allah and the Last Day'

steps.m: to come later, to be last

steps.n: The root أخر meaning 'late/last' gets an ا after the first letter to make it mean 'that which is last/final'
Adding ال makes it specific - 'the last' instead of just any 'last'. The ِ at the end shows it belongs to 'Day' in the phrase 'the Last Day'
The basic demonstrative pronoun ذا (this) gets an alif above it (ذٰ) to show distance, like pointing to something far away
Adding لِ makes the word point to something even further away, like saying 'that over there' instead of just 'this'
Adding كَ shows you're talking to someone, like saying 'that one there' to someone specific
Connected the root letters and added vowel marks to make it into a noun. This word works with أَحْسَنُ (better) to compare two good things.
Added double dammah to show this is any good thing (not a specific one)
Added vowels (fatha-damma-fatha) to make it mean 'it became good/beautiful'
Changed to comparative/superlative form by adding أ at start and changing vowels
Added و at beginning to connect to previous phrase. This word describes تَأْوِيلًا (interpretation) in the next word.

steps.m: to return something to its origin or explain its meaning

steps.n: Added vowels to connect the root letters into a basic past-tense verb
Changed the verb into a noun meaning 'the act or result of interpreting'
Added tanween alif to show this word is being acted upon in the sentence
This is the basic negation word that changes a present tense verb to past tense negative
Adding أَ at the beginning turns this into a question that expects the answer 'yes'. For example, أَلَمْ تَرَ means 'Did you not see?' expecting 'Yes, I did see.'
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Added تَ at the beginning to show 'you' are doing the action, and the verb changes to present tense
The ى at the end falls off because أَلَمْ makes verbs shorter (like when we say 'don't see' instead of 'you see')

This is a basic preposition that shows direction or destination. In this ayah, it connects the verb 'seek judgment' (يَتَحَاكَمُوٓا۟) with 'false deities' (ٱلطَّاغُوتِ), showing they want to take their disputes to false judges.
Added ين to make it refer to multiple people
Added ٱلّ at the beginning to make it specific and point to particular people. This word describes the people who يَزْعُمُونَ (claim) in this ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha to create past tense verb
Changes from past to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing middle vowel to ُ

steps.m: they claim falsely (referring to the hypocrites mentioned in the context)
steps.n: Added masculine plural suffix ون to show multiple people doing the action. In this ayah, it refers to the hypocrites who falsely claim to believe.
This is a particle that connects to يَزْعُمُونَ (they claim) to show what follows is the content of their claim

steps.m: that they (the subject of يَزْعُمُونَ's claim)
steps.n: Adds هُمْ to show who the claim is about - refers back to ٱلَّذِينَ (those who) from earlier in the verse
Added alif after first letter to strengthen the meaning from just trusting to believing
Added وا at the end to show many people did this action
Basic relative pronoun meaning 'what'
Added the preposition بِ at the beginning to show this word connects to something using 'with'
The alif is written long (آ) at the end of words in the Quran to show proper pronunciation
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Changed from active (he sent) to passive (was sent) by adding أُ and changing vowels inside the word
Basic preposition showing direction or movement towards something

steps.m: to you (referring to Prophet Muhammad ﷺ)
steps.n: Added ك at the end to show who is being addressed, with ي helping connect the sounds. This is referring to Prophet Muhammad ﷺ in this ayah.
Added fatha to show proper pronunciation
Added و with fatha at the beginning to connect this word with what came before it, like saying 'and' in English
Extended the alif at the end to show it's being paused on when reading. This doesn't change the meaning.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb

steps.m: was sent down (object of ءَامَنُوا - they believed in)
steps.n: Changed to passive voice by adding أُ at start and changing middle vowel to kasra (ِ). This shows the action was done to something rather than by something. In this ayah, this is what they claimed to believe in.
This is a preposition that shows where something starts or comes from. Here it connects with قَبْلِكَ (before you) to mean 'from before you'
Added fatha and sukun to make it into a noun meaning 'before'. This word is in the genitive case because it comes after the word مِن (from) in the ayah.
Added the pronoun ك (you) at the end with kasra before it to show possession. The whole phrase مِن قَبْلِكَ means 'from before you'.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the past tense verb
Added يُ at start to show present tense. The middle و changes to ي because it's between two vowels (this is a common pattern with و in the middle of words)

steps.m: they want (referring to those who claim they believe)
steps.n: Added ون to show multiple people doing the action. This refers back to الَّذِينَ (those who) from earlier in the verse.

This is a connecting word that joins two ideas together. Here it connects 'they want' (يُرِيدُونَ) with what they want 'to seek judgment' (يَتَحَاكَمُوٓا۟). It's like the word 'that' in English when you say 'I want that you help me.'

steps.m: to make decisions, to judge between right and wrong

steps.n: Root letters got fatha marks to make past tense 'he' form
Adding alif makes it about two people seeking judgment together

steps.m: to seek judgment from each other
steps.n: Adding ت creates reflexive/reciprocal meaning between parties
Changes from past to present tense by adding يَ at the start

steps.m: they seek judgment from each other (in the context of يُرِيدُونَ أَن - they want to)
steps.n: The وا shows multiple people, and the final form is subjunctive because it follows أَن (that/to)

This is a preposition that shows direction or destination. In this ayah, it connects the verb 'seek judgment' (يَتَحَاكَمُوٓا۟) with 'false deities' (ٱلطَّاغُوتِ), showing they want to take their disputes to false judges.
Added ا after first root letter ط and vowel marks to show who does the action
Added وت at the end to create a special noun meaning something that is worshipped falsely or followed in evil

steps.m: the false god or evil power
steps.n: Added ال (the) at beginning. The ل combines with ط to make it stronger (shown by ّ)

steps.m: to the false god (object of يَتَحَاكَمُوٓا۟)
steps.n: Added ِ at the end because it comes after إِلَى (to) in the ayah. This word is what they want to seek judgment from, which is wrong because they were commanded to reject it.
Added vowel marks to show exact pronunciation. This word emphasizes certainty about what follows.

Added و with fatha at the beginning to connect this with the previous statement. This combination emphasizes that they were definitely commanded, connecting to the verb أُمِرُوٓا۟ (they were commanded).
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Changed vowels to show that someone received the order rather than gave it
Added وا at the end to show that many people received the order

This is a connecting word that joins two ideas together. Here it connects 'they want' (يُرِيدُونَ) with what they want to do 'seek judgment' (يَتَحَاكَمُوٓا۟), like saying 'they want that they seek judgment' in English.

steps.m: to reject or cover up truth
