steps.m: to pay compensation or blood money

steps.n: Makes the word indefinite (a/an)
Added و with fatha at the beginning to mean 'and'

steps.m: to hand over, submit, surrender peacefully

steps.n: Doubling the middle letter makes the meaning stronger - like fully submitting
Adding مُ at the beginning shows this is something receiving the action rather than doing it
Added feminine marker ة because this word describes دِيَةٌ (blood money) which is feminine
Added ٌ to show this is indefinite and in the nominative case
Basic preposition showing direction or destination. In this ayah, it connects to أَهْلِهِۦٓ (his family) to show the blood money should be delivered to the victim's family.
Added an alif maksura (ى with a small mark above it) at the end. This is just a spelling variation that doesn't change the meaning.
Added a small vertical line (ٓ) above the word. This mark helps with pronunciation but doesn't change the meaning.

steps.m: to belong, to be familiar with

steps.n: Basic noun form showing the people who belong together
Added kasra to show this family belongs to someone

steps.m: his family (referring to the slain believer)
steps.n: Added هِ to show the family belongs to him (the slain believer mentioned earlier in the verse)

steps.m: except (excludes خَطَـًٔا from the prohibition of killing in يَقْتُلَ)
steps.n: This is a basic exception word that excludes what comes after it (accidental killing) from what came before (the prohibition of killing a believer). In this ayah, it's saying a believer cannot kill another believer EXCEPT by accident.

steps.m: except (excludes خَطَـًٔا from the prohibition of killing in يَقْتُلَ)
steps.n: The alif at the end is lengthened for emphasis in pronunciation, but doesn't change the meaning

steps.m: that (introduces what يَصَّدَّقُوا they give charity)
steps.n: This is a basic particle that connects parts of a sentence, similar to how 'that' connects ideas in English. Here it introduces what the people might do (give charity).

steps.m: that (introduces what يَصَّدَّقُوا they give charity)
steps.n: The fatha (َ) on أ shows this is the basic form of the word, not a variation. It helps distinguish it from similar-looking particles.
Doubling the middle letter makes the meaning stronger and more emphatic
ي at start shows it's happening now and the doer is 'he'
وا shows multiple people (they) are doing the action
Basic conditional particle that introduces a hypothetical situation
Added فَ at beginning to connect this condition with the previous statement, showing sequence or result
Basic root letters got vowel marks to make it a past tense verb meaning 'he was'

steps.m: he was/existed (describing a state in the past)
steps.n: The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a special type of verb with a weak middle letter. This is just a spelling change and doesn't affect the meaning.

This is a basic preposition that connects words to show where something comes from or what group it belongs to. Here it connects 'he was' (كَانَ) to 'a people' (قَوْمٍ) to show which group someone belongs to.

steps.m: to stand up, rise, or stay firm

steps.n: Changes the action of standing into a group of people who stand together as one unit
Added the tanween kasra (ٍ) at the end to show this group belongs to or is connected to something else (in this case, an enemy)

steps.m: to be hostile, to act as an enemy

steps.n: Changes the word into an adjective describing someone who shows hostility
Adding shadda makes the meaning stronger/more intense

steps.m: an intense enemy (of لَّكُمْ - for you)
steps.n: The word got two small lines under it (kasratayn) to show it's 'an enemy' instead of just 'enemy'. It connects to لَّكُمْ (for you) in the ayah
Starting with the basic preposition ل which shows direction or purpose
The shadda (ّ) shows that this merges with a similar sound from the previous word (assimilation)
Added كُمْ to show this applies to multiple people being addressed
Added vowel marks to show correct pronunciation of the basic pronoun

steps.m: and he (who is مِن قَوْمٍ عَدُوٍّ - from an enemy people)
steps.n: Added the connecting particle و with fatha at the beginning to join this clause with what came before
Added مُ at the start to show who does the action, like changing 'trust' to 'truster'. The ؤْ comes from the original أ with a special mark to show how to pronounce it.

steps.m: a believer (who is being described in this ayah)
steps.n: Added ٌ at the end to show this is describing someone in general, like saying 'a believer' instead of 'the believer'. This word is being described in this part of the ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a basic past tense verb
Doubling the middle letter makes the meaning stronger and more thorough
Changes from verb (to free) into noun meaning 'the act of freeing'
Adds 'and' or 'then' to connect this word to what came before it
Adds the nominative case marker to show this is the subject or in a default state

steps.m: neck (literally: the part that is watched/guarded)
steps.n: The neck is called رَقَبَة because it's a vulnerable part that needs watching/guarding. This concrete meaning then extended to mean 'slave' since slaves were seen as 'necks' to be owned.

steps.m: a slave/neck (object of فَتَحْرِيرُ - freeing of)
steps.n: Added tanwin kasra to show this is the object being freed. This word is connected to فَتَحْرِيرُ (freeing of) in the ayah.

steps.m: to make safe, to cause to believe
steps.n: Changed the root to show belief by adding آ at the beginning. This makes it mean 'to believe' instead of just 'to feel safe'.
Changed the word to show who does the believing by adding مُ at the start. This word describes رَقَبَةٍ (slave/captive) in the ayah.
Added ة to show this is describing a female slave/captive

steps.m: a believing female (in the genitive case)
steps.n: The ٍ shows this word is being described by or connected to another word
This is a compound particle made of و (and) plus إن (if)
Adding fathas creates basic past tense form
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a weak letter in the past tense. This is a common change in Arabic that happens automatically for pronunciation.
This is a basic preposition showing source or origin. In this ayah, it connects the word قَوْمٍ (people) to كَانَ (was), showing who someone was from.
Connected the root letters with fatha and sukun to form a noun meaning a group that stands together as one
Added tanween kasra to show this is an indefinite noun being possessed or affected by something. In this ayah, it shows these are people who have a specific relationship with others mentioned.
Forms the common preposition meaning 'between'
Adds second person masculine plural possessive pronoun suffix to indicate 'between you all'

steps.m: to separate or be distinct from

steps.n: Added fatha on ب and sukun on ي to make it mean 'between'. This changes it from an action (separating) to showing the space or relationship between things.
هم is added to show it means 'between them'
Adding و (and) at the start connects this word to what came before it

steps.m: to bind firmly, make secure, trust

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha to create basic past tense verb
Added مِي at start and changed vowels to make a noun meaning a formal promise or agreement that binds people together
Added shadda on م and tanween dammah at end to show indefiniteness. The alif is written as ٰ (dagger alif)

steps.m: to compensate or pay blood money
steps.n: This root relates to paying compensation, especially for injuries or death
Changes from verb (to compensate) into noun (compensation)

steps.m: a compensation payment (that must be paid to أَهْلِهِ)
steps.n: Adding ٌ shows this is an undefined compensation payment that must be paid to the victim's family (أَهْلِهِ) as mentioned in the ayah
Adding shadda makes the action more complete or intensive
Adding مُ at the start changes it from 'to hand over' to 'the thing that was handed over' - like changing 'deliver' to 'delivered item'
Adding ة makes this describe a feminine thing that is handed over
Word is complete with all needed parts - مُ prefix, shadda, and ة ending
This is a basic preposition showing direction or destination. It connects what comes before it to what comes after it.
Added vowel marks to show exact pronunciation: kasra under إ and fatha over ل
Added small alif (ٰ) to show that the final ى is pronounced like a long a sound. This preposition connects to the word أَهْلِهِ (his family) that comes after it.

steps.m: to be related to, belong to family

steps.n: Forms the basic noun meaning family/people
Shows this word is connected to the preposition إِلَىٰٓ (to) that comes before it
Added هِ (his) at the end to show it's his family. The word comes after إِلَىٰٓ (to) in the sentence.
Added a shadda (double letter) to make it mean 'he set free' instead of just the basic idea of freedom
Changed verb into a noun meaning 'the act of freeing'
Added damma to show this word is doing the action in the sentence
Added و at beginning to connect this with previous statement
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form basic past tense verb

steps.m: a neck/slave (from the idea of watching over someone)
steps.n: The word for 'neck' became used for 'slave' because masters would watch over their slaves, like guarding their necks

steps.m: a slave (being acted upon in the sentence)
steps.n: The word gets a special ending mark (tanween kasra) because it's receiving the action of 'freeing' in this ayah

steps.m: to trust, feel safe, or believe in something

steps.n: Changed the root meaning to show that someone is making someone else believe or trust
Creates a word for someone who does the action of believing. This word describes رَقَبَةٍ (slave/captive) in this ayah.

steps.m: female believer (describing رَقَبَةٍ which is feminine)
steps.n: Adds ة to match رَقَبَةٍ which is feminine
Added a kasra (small i sound) at the end because it follows رَقَبَةٍ which has kasra
Basic interrogative/conditional particle that asks about or refers to people
Added فَ at the beginning to show this is a result of what came before. This connects to the verb يَجِدْ (finds) that comes after it.

The letter ل gets doubled (shadda) because it comes right after the word فَمَن
This is the basic concept of finding or getting something

steps.n: Changed the word from an action (to fast) into a thing (fasting)
Changed و to ي because it's easier to pronounce
Added فَ to mean 'then/so' and ُ to show this is the subject doing something in the sentence

Added يْنِ to show exactly two months. This word follows صِيَامُ (fasting) in the ayah, showing what is being fasted.
Connected the root letters with basic past tense vowels
Adding alif shows two or more people following each other
Added مُ at start and تَ in middle to show something happening one after another in order
Added dual ending to match the two months being described
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and make it a noun
Adding ة at the end turns the action of 'turning back' into a single instance/act of repentance
Added tanween to show this is the object being acted upon. In this ayah, the fasting is being done as repentance.
This is the basic preposition that shows where something starts or comes from
The م gets a shadda (double mark) because it comes after a word ending in a similar sound
The fatha is added at the end to connect it smoothly to the next word (اللَّهِ)
Changed to the proper name Allah by adding ال and merging letters. This is a unique word that became the name for God in Arabic.

Added basic past tense vowels to show this is talking about something that already happened

Added ال to specify this is THE one God, and the hamza was dropped because this is a special word
Added shadda on ل to emphasize it, changed initial alif to wasl form, and added damma to show this is the subject of كَانَ (was)
Added ي between second and third letters with vowels to show this is a strong, permanent quality
Added ًا at the end to show this word is describing Allah's quality mentioned in وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ (and Allah is/was)

The ًا ending shows this word is describing Allah's attribute of wisdom, working with كان (was/is) to say 'Allah is perfectly wise'

steps.m: he kills (in the condition وَمَن - whoever)

steps.m: to be/feel secure, safe, or trusted

Added ًا at the end to show this word is receiving the action of killing. The ً marks show it's any believer in general, not a specific one.
Added basic vowels to connect the letters together
Changed from a verb (to do) into a noun (one who does)
Changes to show it describes HOW something was done (adds -an ending) + first letter is doubled for pronunciation

steps.m: to compensate or give what is due

Changed the verb into a noun meaning the result of the action
Changed ending to show this is the subject (the thing giving the reward)
Added 'his' to show who the reward belongs to
Added فَ to connect this with the previous statement. This word is the subject, with جَهَنَّمُ (Hell) being what's said about it.

The ى changes to ي with sukun when adding a pronoun suffix. This is just a spelling change that doesn't affect the meaning.
Added هِ (hi) at the end to mean 'him'. This shows who the preposition is affecting.

Added هُ (him) to show who receives the curse
Added وَ (and) to connect this action with Allah's anger mentioned before (غَضِبَ)
Added vowel marks (fat-ha) to make it 'he prepared' in past tense
Adding أ at start makes it mean 'to make something/someone prepared'
Added و at beginning to connect this word to what came before it
Added fatha to help with pronunciation
Added هُ at the end to show this is 'for him'. This word connects to عَذَابًا (punishment) in this ayah, showing who the punishment is for.
Added ۥ which is just a pronunciation mark showing the هُ is pronounced clearly
Added vowel marks to connect the letters together and make a past tense verb.
Adding an alif between the second and third root letters turns this into a noun describing the result of the action.
The ًا at the end shows this word is receiving the action of the verb أَعَدَّ (prepared) in the phrase 'He has prepared for him a punishment.'
Added ي between ظ and م, with َ on ع and ِ on ظ to show this describes a strong quality
Added ًا at the end to show this describes the word عَذَابًا (punishment) in the ayah

Starting with the basic relative pronoun and adding ي to make it a complete word
Adding ين to make it plural - refers to multiple people instead of one

This is a basic time word that refers to a specific moment in the past
Adding ا at the end changes this from referring to a past time to referring to a future time or condition. In this ayah, it introduces the condition 'when you go forth in Allah's path'

Adding the vowel marks shows this is a complete past tense verb

Basic preposition showing location or movement within something. Here it connects 'you went forth' (ضَرَبْتُمْ) with 'the path' (سَبِيلِ) to show where the going forth happens.

Added kasra at the end to show this word is connected to what comes after it (Allah)

Changed to the specific name for the One True God by adding ال and merging it with the word
Added emphasis by doubling the ل letter and changed initial alif to wasl form
Added kasra at the end to show possession or belonging
Connected root letters and added shadda to show intensive meaning
Added ت at beginning to show doing the action to oneself
Changed from 'he' to 'you all' by adding -uu ending to make it a command for a group
Added فَ to show this command follows from previous context

Added تَ at beginning to show 'you' are doing the action

Changed the ending to تَ to show 'you' as the subject. The ي is dropped because it's a weak letter.
Adding vowels to make it a past tense verb about feeling secure

Added shadda to emphasize the L sound, showing the merging of the ل from ال with the ل in the word

Added basic past tense vowels to connect the letters

Added ُ to show this is the subject doing something
Added vowels and ي to make a word meaning 'much' or 'many'

Added tanween dammah (double damma) to show this is indefinite and in the nominative case

Added ذٰ (that) to specify what is being compared to

Added كَ at the end to complete the demonstrative expression
Command form (imperative) meaning 'Be!' - made by adding damma and sukun
Added past tense marker ت with damma to show 'you' or 'I'

Basic preposition showing where something comes from or what group it belongs to
Added shadda (doubled م) to emphasize the meaning. This often happens when the word before ends with a similar sound.
Adding vowel marks to make the basic noun form meaning 'before'

steps.m: and so he bestowed favor (on عَلَيْكُمْ)

Changed the root into a noun form meaning 'a deity' by adding vowel marks
Changed to the proper name Allah by adding ال with a special spelling, doubling the ل with shadda, and adding ُ to show this is the subject doing something in this sentence
This is a preposition showing something being on top of or over something else
The ى changes to يْ to prepare for connecting to the next part. This is just a spelling change that doesn't affect the meaning.
Added كُمْ at the end to show this applies to multiple people being spoken to
The ت at the beginning and doubled ي show doing the action thoroughly to oneself
The وا ending makes this a command for a group

This is the basic form of the particle that connects parts of sentences

steps.m: Allah (as the object being known about)
steps.n: The word 'Allah' gets a special mark (shadda) to make the L sound stronger, and ends with a fatha mark to show Allah is being talked about in the sentence

steps.m: he/it was (describes Allah's state of being خَبِيرًا/aware)
steps.n: The middle و becomes ا and we add fathas to show this happened in the past. This word tells us about Allah's continuous state of being aware of our actions.

steps.m: with what/with that which (relates to تَعْمَلُونَ)
steps.n: Added بِ at the beginning to show this is about doing something 'with' or 'by means of' what follows. This connects to the verb تَعْمَلُونَ (you do/are doing) in the ayah.
Adding vowels to make it a past tense verb
Changed from past to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels
Changed from 'he' to 'you' by replacing يَ with تَ at the start
Added ونَ to show multiple people are doing the action

steps.m: All-Aware (describing how Allah is with what تَعْمَلُونَ you do)

Adding ون makes it plural (many people). The alif is written as ٰ when it comes after ق. This word is being compared to المجاهدون (the strivers) in the verse.
This is a basic preposition that shows something comes from, is part of, or is selected from something else
Added fatha at the end to help connect smoothly to the next word ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (the believers). This is a pronunciation helper that doesn't change the meaning.
Connected the root letters together
Added مُ at start to make it mean 'one who' and added vowels to make it a doer of the action (like changing 'believe' to 'believer')
Added ين at the end to show there are many believers
Added ال at beginning to make it specific and َ at end to show it's being acted upon in the sentence
Basic noun form showing something that is different
Added damma at the end to show this word is the subject of يَسْتَوِى (are equal) in this ayah
This is a special word used to show possession or belonging. It's always used with something after it to show who/what possesses something.
The ى at the end shows this word is connected to what comes after it. Here it's connected to 'disability/harm' to mean 'those who have disability/harm'.
Adding vowel marks to show it's a noun meaning 'harm'
Adding kasra at the end shows this word is 'of harm' or 'harm's'

steps.m: in (connects سَبِيلِ with what comes before it)

Adding ي makes it mean 'path' - a common pattern in Arabic
Added kasra at the end to show this word connects to Allah (اللَّهِ) that comes after it, meaning "path of Allah"
Changed to the proper name Allah by adding ال and merging letters. This is a unique word that became the proper name for God in Arabic.
Added kasra at the end to show possession of the word سَبِيلِ (path) that comes before it in the ayah. Also added shadda on ل and changed initial alif to wasl form.
Adding the fatha vowel mark helps show this is an active form
Changed to broken plural form to show multiple properties or types of wealth
Added kasra at end to show this word belongs to or is connected to something

steps.n: This is a connecting word (particle) that shows options or alternatives. In this ayah, it connects two valid reasons for Muslims to put down their weapons during prayer: rain or sickness.

Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb

Added damma at end to show this is the subject of sentence
Changed to the proper name Allah by adding ال and merging letters. This is a unique word that became the name for God in Islam.
Added kasra at the end to show possession, shadda on ل, and changed initial alif. This word shows who the grace (فَضْلُ) belongs to in this ayah.
This is a basic preposition showing something being above or having authority over something else. Think of it like showing where something sits 'on top of' something else.
The ى changes to ي before adding a suffix. This is just a spelling change and doesn't change the meaning.

steps.m: upon you (object of أَنزَلَ - He sent down)
steps.n: Added ك at the end to show this is happening to 'you'. In this ayah, this word shows that Allah sent down the Book upon you (Muhammad ﷺ).
Added ي between the last two root letters and vowels to make it into a describing word

steps.m: great/magnificent (describing Allah's favor in this ayah)
steps.n: The ًا at the end shows this word is describing Allah's favor (فَضْلُ) mentioned earlier in the ayah
Basic negative particle that negates the noun خَيْرَ (good) that comes after it in this ayah

steps.m: there is absolutely no (negates خَيْرَ - good)
steps.n: The shadda (doubled L sound) makes the negation stronger, like saying 'absolutely no' instead of just 'no'
Changes from verb 'to be good' into the noun 'good/goodness'

steps.m: good (after لَّا meaning 'there is no good')
steps.n: The fatha at the end shows this word is affected by لَّا (there is no). This grammatical structure is used to say 'there is no good in...'
This is a basic preposition showing location or containment. It has no root since it's a primitive word.
Added ى at the end to complete the preposition. In this ayah, it connects 'much' (كَثِيرٍ) with 'their private conversation' (نَّجْوَىٰهُمْ).
Added ي between the last two letters and vowels to make it into a describing word
Added the tanwin kasra (ٍ) at the end to show this describes what comes after it in the ayah
This is a basic word that shows something comes from somewhere or belongs to something, like saying 'from their private conversation' in this verse
The doubled م (shown by ّ ) makes the meaning stronger, like saying 'directly from' or 'specifically from'
Changed from basic root 'to whisper' into a noun meaning 'the secret talk' by adding special vowels
Added emphasis with shadda and an alif to extend the sound
Added هُمْ to show it belongs to them
Added shadda to emphasize the meaning. This is a particle used for making exceptions, like saying 'There is no good in most of their secret talks EXCEPT...'
Added alif at the end to help connect this word to what comes after it. In this ayah, it connects to 'whoever commands charity...'

steps.m: whoever (introduces the condition in this ayah)
steps.n: This is a conditional pronoun that means 'whoever' and introduces a condition in this ayah. It connects two parts: the condition (whoever commands charity...) and its result (We will give them a great reward).
Connected the letters together with fatha marks to show this happened in the past. This word tells us what 'whoever' (مَنْ) did in the ayah.
Changes from the basic idea of 'giving' into a noun meaning 'one specific gift or donation'
The tanwin (double kasra) shows this is any charitable gift (not a specific one). It has kasra because it follows the preposition بِ.

steps.m: by/through a charitable gift (connected to أَمَرَ - commanded)
steps.n: The بِ shows this charitable gift is the means by which the command/encouragement happens. This whole phrase describes how the commanding/encouraging in أَمَرَ should happen.
This is a conjunction that connects two alternative good deeds mentioned in the ayah: giving charity (صَدَقَة) or doing good (مَعْرُوف). It shows these are equally valid options.

steps.m: to recognize or know something as good/acceptable

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Added مَ at the start and changed middle vowels to make it mean 'something that is known/recognized as good' instead of the action of recognizing
Added two dots under the last letter to show this word is receiving an action or being described
This is a connecting word that gives equal choices between 'charity' (صَدَقَةٍ) and 'good' (مَعْرُوفٍ) in this ayah. It works like 'or' in English when listing options.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Added إِ at start and ا after ل to change from 'he fixed' to 'fixing/making peace' (turns action into a noun)

steps.m: any act of making peace (one of the good deeds mentioned with صَدَقَةٍ and مَعْرُوفٍ)
steps.n: Added tanwin kasra at the end to show this is one of several good deeds being talked about in the verse, along with giving charity (صَدَقَةٍ) and doing good (مَعْرُوفٍ)
This root relates to separation or distinction between things
Adding vowels turns the root into a common preposition meaning 'between'

steps.m: to be close and friendly with others
steps.n: This root relates to human companionship and society
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'people' by adding a long 'aa' sound
Added ال to show we're talking about specific people, and doubled the ن with shadda
Added kasra at the end because this word is connected to another word that affects it (shows possession or description)
This is a relative pronoun that refers to any person who does something. Here it connects to the verb يَفْعَلْ (does/will do) in the next part of the ayah.
Added و at the beginning to connect this part with what came before it, like saying 'and' in English
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Changed to present/future tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels

steps.m: he does/will do (in a conditional statement)
steps.n: Changed to conditional form because it comes after مَن (whoever) in the ayah
This is the basic demonstrative pronoun for pointing to something nearby
The alif is written with a special mark (ٰ) to show medium distance
Adding ل with kasra (ِ) makes it point to something farther away
The ك at the end makes it specifically point to something mentioned or known (in this case, referring back to the good deeds mentioned before)

steps.m: to seek or strive for something
steps.n: This root relates to seeking or desiring something
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a basic past tense verb
Added ٱ at start and ت after first letter to show doing action for oneself with extra effort
Changed verb 'he sought' into noun 'seeking' by adding اء at end
Added fatha at end to show this is the object being acted upon
This root relates to pleasure and satisfaction
Basic noun form showing the concept of pleasure
Added مَ at the beginning to show 'source/place of' something, and changed و to ا at the end which is a common pattern for this type of word
Added ات to make it plural (feminine form)
Added ِ to show this belongs to Allah (اللَّهِ) in this ayah
Added shadda to emphasize the L sound, showing the special nature of this divine name
Added kasra at the end to show this is possessed by the word مَرْضَاتِ (pleasure), meaning 'pleasure of Allah'
This is a particle that shows something will happen in the future. It's stronger and more distant in the future than just using سَ.
Added فَ at the beginning to show this happens as a result of or following something else. In this ayah, it shows what will happen after someone does good deeds seeking Allah's pleasure.
This root relates to coming or bringing something
Connected the root letters together to form the basic verb
Added نُ at start to show 'we' are doing the action. The ؤ is used because of the hamza in the root.

steps.m: we give him (referring to the reward mentioned after)
steps.n: Added هِ at the end to show who receives the giving. This connects to أَجْرًا (reward) mentioned right after this word.
This root relates to payment or reward for work
Root letters turned into a noun meaning 'reward' by adding specific vowel marks
Added tanween alif to show this is the object being given in the phrase 'We will give him a great reward'
Added vowels and ي to make an adjective showing a strong quality
Added tanween alif to show this is describing the reward (أَجْرًا) mentioned in the ayah
This is a combination of two particles: و (and) + من (who/whoever)
Doubled middle radical shows intensity or repetition
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a basic past tense verb
Adding alif after first letter shows the action is done against someone else
Adding يُ at start shows action happening now. Changed middle vowel to kasra.
Changed final vowel to kasra because this word comes after مَن (whoever) in a conditional phrase

steps.m: to send or deliver a message
steps.n: This form makes it a plural noun meaning 'messengers'
The و makes it mean someone who does this action as their main role
Added ال to make it specific and doubled the ر with shadda
Added fatha at the end to show this word is being acted upon by the verb يُشَاقِقِ (opposes/differs with)
This is a preposition that shows where something starts from or comes from. Here it connects with the word بَعْدِ (after) to mean 'from after'
Connected the root letters and added vowel marks to create the basic noun form
Added kasra at the end to show this word follows مِنۢ (from). Together they mean 'from after' or simply 'after'.
This particle means 'what' here and connects to the verb تَوَلَّىٰ (he turned to), making the phrase mean 'what he turned to'. It helps turn the action into something that can be referred to.
Basic meaning of clarity or separation
Adding shadda makes the meaning active - someone/something is doing the clarifying

steps.m: to become clear (after guidance has become clear to him)
steps.n: Adding تَ at the beginning shows the clarity happens by itself, without someone doing it. This word relates to 'guidance' (الْهُدَىٰ) becoming clear to someone.
Added fatha to help with pronunciation
Added هُ at the end to show who the action is for. This word connects to يُشَاقِقِ (opposes) to show who is being opposed.

steps.m: to guide, show the right way
steps.n: The last root letter ي changes to و because it's at the end of a past tense verb
Adding damma (ُ) to first letter starts turning it into a noun meaning 'guidance'
Adding ى and tanween makes it an indefinite noun meaning 'guidance'
Added ال to make it specific guidance. The alif at the end is written with a small mark above (ٰ) to show it's pronounced like a regular alif.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb
Changes from past to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels
Adding ت with shadda and changing the vowel makes the meaning more intense and shows doing the action thoroughly
Adds و at beginning to connect this action with what came before it
Adds sukun at the end to show this is part of a conditional statement

steps.m: to change, to be different from something

steps.n: Connected the root letters together with fatha on first letter and sukun on second letter to create a noun meaning 'other than'. This word is being acted upon by يَتَّبِعْ (follows) in this ayah.

steps.m: other than (object of يَتَّبِعْ, owned by سَبِيلِ)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show this word is being acted upon by the verb يَتَّبِعْ (follows) and is connected to سَبِيلِ (path) in a possession relationship.

steps.m: to travel a path or way

steps.n: Adds vowel marks to show this is describing a path or route
Adding ي makes this a permanent path/way rather than temporary condition
Added kasra at the end to show this word is connected to 'the believers' that comes after it
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the past tense verb
Added مُ at start and changed middle vowels to make it mean 'someone who believes' (active participle)
Added ين at the end to show there are many believers
Added ٱلْ at start to make it specific and َ at end because it's possessed by the word 'path' in سَبِيلِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (path of the believers)
Added shadda to strengthen the meaning and show intensity of the action
Added نُ at start to show 'we' and changed vowels to show present tense

steps.m: we will put him in charge (of ما تَوَلَّىٰ - what he chose)
steps.n: Added هِ with extension (ۦ) at the end to show 'him' as the object. This connects to ما تَوَلَّىٰ (what he chose) in the next part of the ayah.

steps.m: what (that which was made clear to him)
steps.n: This is a relative pronoun particle that connects two parts of the sentence. Here it refers back to 'that which' was made clear, connecting to تَبَيَّنَ (became clear). It helps explain what became clear to the person.
Connected the root letters with basic past tense vowels
Doubling the middle letter makes the action stronger or more intense
Adding ت at start shows the person is doing the action to themselves
The word gets a small alif (ى) at the end to show it's a past tense verb meaning 'turned away'
Basic meaning of heating or burning something
Added vowels to make basic past tense verb
Changed to present/future tense with 'we' as subject by adding نُ at start and changing vowels
Added هِ to show who receives the action (him)

steps.m: and we burn/will burn him (connected to نُوَلِّهِۦ)
steps.n: Added وَ to connect with previous statement and ۦ to show proper pronunciation

steps.m: Hell (object of وَنُصْلِهِۦ - and We will burn him in)
steps.n: Connected the letters and added vowels to form the proper noun. This word is the object of the verb وَنُصْلِهِۦ (and We will burn him in) in this ayah.

steps.m: Hell (object of وَنُصْلِهِۦ - and We will burn him in)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show this is the object being acted upon by the verb وَنُصْلِهِۦ (and We will burn him in).
Connected the letters and changed و to ا because it's in the middle. Added fatha marks to show past tense.
Added an extra alif to show the long vowel sound more clearly
Added ت with sukun to show this is about something feminine in the past

steps.m: and it (feminine) became bad (describing مَصِيرًا - destination)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this with the previous part of the sentence. This word is describing مَصِيرًا (destination) in the ayah.

steps.m: to arrive at a destination or reach an end point

steps.n: Added مَ at the beginning and ِ in the middle to create a noun meaning 'the place where something ends up'

steps.m: a destination (as the object being described)
steps.n: Added ًا at the end to show this word is being described or acted upon in the sentence
This is the basic form of the particle that introduces conditions or statements

steps.m: indeed/verily (emphasizes that اللَّهَ does not forgive)
steps.n: Adding shadda changes this from a conditional particle to an emphasis particle. It makes what comes after it stronger and more certain.
Changed to the proper name Allah by adding ال and merging letters. This is a unique word in Arabic that always refers to The One True God.

steps.m: Allah (as the object of يُشْرِكْ - associates partners with)
steps.n: Added shadda on ل to show emphasis, changed initial alif to wasl form, and added fatha at end to show this is the object being acted upon in the phrase 'associates partners with Allah'
This is a negative particle that makes the verb after it (يَغْفِرُ) mean 'does not forgive'. It's like adding 'not' before a verb in English.
Added fatha mark on ل to show correct pronunciation
Connected the root letters with fatha to create past tense verb
Changes from past tense (he forgave) to present tense (he forgives) by adding يَ at the start
The ُ (damma) on the last letter indicates present tense nominative case
This is a connecting word that joins two ideas together. In this ayah, it connects 'Allah does not forgive' with 'that partners are associated with Him', making one complete thought.
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the root letters

steps.m: to be made a partner (with اللَّهِ)
steps.n: Changed to passive voice present tense by adding يُ at start and changing vowels. This shows something being done rather than someone doing it. In this ayah, it refers to partners being associated with Allah.
Basic preposition showing connection or means

steps.m: with him/it (referring to يُشْرَكَ - being associated)
steps.n: Added pronoun هِ to show who/what is being referred to. This connects back to the act of associating partners mentioned in يُشْرَكَ.
Added small alif (ۦ) after هِ as a pronunciation guide to show the long vowel sound
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create past tense verb
Adds 'and' to connect this verb with the previous statement about what Allah does not forgive
This is a relative pronoun that connects to the word ذٰلِكَ (that) in this ayah. It helps explain that Allah forgives what is less than shirk.
The word became a preposition that means 'other than' or 'less than'
Added damma and fatha to make it a preposition. In this ayah, it's used with اللَّهِ to mean 'other than Allah'
The alif is written with a special mark (ٰ) to show this points to something far away instead of close by
Added لِ which helps connect to the next part. This is like a bridge between parts of the word.

steps.m: that (pointing to something far) (refers to what was mentioned about شرك/associating partners with Allah)
steps.n: Added كَ at the end which makes this word specifically point to something mentioned before. In this ayah, it points back to the idea of associating partners with Allah that was just mentioned.
This is a question word that became a conditional word, meaning 'whoever' or 'anyone who'. It's used to talk about any person in general.

steps.m: for whoever (object of يَغْفِرُ - He forgives)
steps.n: Added لِ at the beginning to show this is the person receiving Allah's forgiveness in the phrase 'He forgives whoever'
The root letters were joined together and vowels were added to make the past tense 'he' form. The middle letter ي turned into a long ا with hamza (ء)
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and ُ at end. The alif is written as آ to show it's a long vowel.
This is a question word that became a conditional particle meaning 'whoever' when used with a verb after it. Here it introduces the condition 'whoever associates partners with Allah'.

Added وَ at the beginning to connect this condition with the previous statement. This word starts the condition, and the person it refers to is the one doing the action in يُشْرِكْ (associates partners).
Basic meaning of the root letters
Adding vowels makes this a complete past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يُ at start, changing vowels, and adding sukun at end to show it's in a conditional phrase
This is the unique name of God in Islam. The word comes from combining ال (the) with إله (deity), but has become a distinct proper noun.
Added بِ at the beginning to show how something is done or the means by which it happens. Added ِ at the end because it follows the preposition بِ.
This is an emphasis particle that strengthens the meaning of what comes after it
Added vowel marks to show correct pronunciation

steps.m: then certainly/indeed (connects to يُشْرِكْ - associates partners)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to show this is a result of what came before. In this ayah, it connects to the verb يُشْرِكْ (associates partners), showing the result of that action.

steps.m: to lose the way/to deviate from the right path

steps.n: Changed from basic root to past tense verb meaning 'he went astray' - works with فَقَدْ to show it definitely happened
When two identical letters come together, they combine with a shadda (ّ) on top. This doesn't change the meaning, just makes it easier to pronounce.

steps.m: he wandered off the right path
steps.n: Connected the root letters and added shadda to show emphasis on middle letter
Changed from 'he went astray' (a verb) to 'going astray' (the name of the action). Like changing 'ran' to 'running'

steps.m: a complete wandering off (emphasizing the verb ضَلَّ)
steps.n: The ًا ending makes it an adverb, showing HOW MUCH they wandered off - it means they went completely astray
Added ي between second and third letters to show this is a strong quality

steps.m: far astray (describes ضَلَّ ضَلٰلًۢا - went astray)
steps.n: Added tanween alif to show this describes how someone went astray. In this ayah, it describes how the person who associates partners with Allah has gone far astray.

steps.m: if (introduces the condition يَدْعُونَ they call)
steps.n: This is a basic particle that shows a condition. In this ayah, it introduces the condition 'if they call' which is followed by what happens in that case.
Added vowels to connect the letters and form past tense. The و changes to ا at the end because it's a weak letter.
Added يَ at start to show present tense, and changed vowels. The ا changes back to و at the end.
This is a basic preposition that shows where something starts from or comes from
When مِن comes before دون (as in دونه 'besides Him'), it emphasizes the meaning of 'other than' or 'instead of'. Here it shows they are calling upon others instead of Allah.

steps.m: to be below or less than

steps.n: Adding the damma vowel mark changes it to mean 'below' or 'lesser'
Added kasra to connect it to the next word
Added pronoun هِۦٓ meaning 'Him' when referring to Allah

steps.m: except (excludes شَيْطٰنًا from what they call upon)
steps.n: This is a basic particle that means 'except'. In this ayah, it shows that they only call upon شَيْطٰنًا (Satan), excluding everything else.

steps.m: except (excludes شَيْطٰنًا from what they call upon)
steps.n: The regular alif (ا) is written as a long alif (آ). This is just a writing style and doesn't change the meaning or pronunciation.
Changed the root letters into a noun meaning 'female' by adding vowels and alif maqsurah (ى) at the end
Changed from singular 'female' to plural 'females' by changing the word pattern - similar to how we say 'girl' to 'girls' in English

steps.m: females (as the object being called upon)
steps.n: Added ًا at the end to show this word is receiving the action of being called upon in the phrase يَدْعُونَ (they call/invoke)
This is a conditional particle that introduces a new condition or statement. In this ayah, it's setting up a condition about who they call upon.

steps.m: and if/indeed (connects this condition about يَدْعُونَ to what came before)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this condition to the previous statement, like saying 'and' in English

steps.m: to call out to someone or invite them

steps.n: The root letters got vowels (fathas) to make it past tense 'he'. The و at the end turned into ا because it's easier to say
Added يَ at start to show it's happening now. The middle letter gets a sukun (ْ) and the last letter gets a damma (ُ). The ا changes back to و.
Added نَ at the end to show multiple people are doing the action
Basic form of the exception particle
Adding shadda emphasizes the exclusion being made

steps.m: except (in this ayah, it excludes female idols from what they should worship)
steps.n: This is not a transformation - إِلَّا is the complete word meaning 'except'. إِلَّ (without alif) is not a valid Arabic word. The note should focus on how إِلَّا is used in this ayah to exclude female idols from what should be worshipped.

steps.m: to be far away, rebel, act defiantly

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha to create the basic past tense verb
Adding ي turns this into a special verb meaning 'to act like/become something'
Added ا after second root letter to create a noun describing one who embodies extreme rebellion
Added ًا at the end to show this is the thing being acted upon (like saying 'I saw a devil')

steps.m: to rebel or go beyond limits

steps.n: Added vowels to connect the root letters into a past-tense verb
Changed the verb into an adjective describing someone who rebels a lot
Added emphasis to show strong rebellion
Added the accusative case ending because this word is describing Satan (شَيْطٰنًا) in the ayah

steps.m: to curse or drive away from mercy

steps.n: Added fathas to connect the letters and show this happened in the past. This word is connected to قَالَ (he said) in this ayah.
The shadda (ّ) on the first letter shows emphasis, making the curse definite and strong

steps.m: he definitely cursed him (subject of قَالَ)
steps.n: Added هُ at the end to show who was cursed (him)
Connected the letters and added vowels to make the word for 'deity'
Added the U mark at the end to show this is the subject

steps.m: he said (after لَّعَنَهُ ٱللَّهُ - Allah cursed him)
steps.n: Added fatha marks to show this happened in the past. The middle letter و changes to ا because it's between two vowels - this is a common change in words with و in the middle.

steps.m: and he said (after لَّعَنَهُ ٱللَّهُ - Allah cursed him)
steps.n: Added و (wa) at the beginning to connect this with the previous statement about Allah cursing him. This shows the sequence of events.
Added ت with shadda to show doing something for oneself
Changed to show I am doing the action in the present time
Added لَ at beginning to emphasize that this will definitely happen
Added نَّ at the end to make it even more certain and emphatic
This is a preposition that shows which group something is being taken from. In this ayah, it shows that something will be taken from among Allah's servants.
Added vowel marks to connect the root letters and make a singular noun meaning one who serves/worships
Changed the internal structure to make it plural, using the pattern with ِ on first letter and ا after second letter
Added kasra at the end to show this word is possessed by something (in this case, it will be possessed by 'you')
Added كَ at the end to show these are your servants. The word comes after مِنْ (from) in the ayah.

steps.m: to set up or establish something firmly

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb
Changes from verb 'to establish' to noun meaning 'a set portion'

steps.m: a share/portion (object of لَأَتَّخِذَنَّ - I will surely take)
steps.n: Added tanween alif to show this is the object being taken in the phrase 'I will surely take a portion'
Connected the root letters with fatha to make a past tense verb
Changed from 'he required' to 'something that was required' - like changing from the action to describing what received the action
Added emphasis to show this is definitely required

steps.m: a firmly assigned portion (object of لَأَتَّخِذَنَّ)
steps.n: Added tanween alif to show this is the object being taken in the phrase "I will surely take...a portion"
Changes from active to passive - 'someone is being led astray'

steps.m: I will definitely make them go astray
steps.n: The نَّ adds strong emphasis ('definitely/surely'), and the word changes from passive ('being led astray') to active ('I will make them go astray')

steps.m: I will definitely make them go astray
steps.n: The لَ at the start adds more emphasis to the promise or oath

steps.m: I will definitely make them go astray
steps.n: هُمْ means 'them' - it shows who is being led astray

steps.m: And I will definitely make them go astray
steps.n: و adds the meaning of 'and' to connect this clause with previous statement

steps.m: to make false promises or delude with false hopes

steps.n: The hamza (أ) at the start plus the doubled middle letter (نّ) changes 'false hopes' into 'I make/give false hopes to others'
The lam (ل) at the start adds 'I will definitely' - making the promise stronger
Added نّ at the end to make a strong promise - like saying 'I WILL DEFINITELY do this!'

steps.m: and I will surely make (others) wish
steps.n: Added conjunction و to connect with previous statement

steps.m: and I will surely make them wish
steps.n: Added object pronoun هم to specify who is being acted upon
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Changed from 'he commanded' to 'I command' by adding alif at start and changing vowels
Added لَ for emphasis
Added نَّ for strong emphasis and future meaning
Added هُمْ to show who is being commanded

steps.m: and I will definitely command them
steps.n: Added وَ to connect this with the previous statement
Added vowels (fatha) to make it past tense 'he cut off'
Added shadda to show intensive cutting
Changed from past to present tense by adding يُ at the start

steps.m: he will definitely cut off intensively
steps.n: Added لَ at start and نَّ at end to make it mean 'he will definitely' do the action

steps.m: and so he will definitely cut off intensively
steps.n: Added فَ to connect this action with previous statements
Changes from a verb about hearing to the plural noun meaning multiple ears
The root letters were connected with the vowel marks 'fatha' (َ) to form a noun meaning livestock - a word that came to represent one of the most important blessings for early Arabs
This form makes the word plural, like changing 'cow' to 'cattle'
Adding ال makes it specific, like changing 'cattle' to 'the cattle'
The ِ at the end shows this word belongs to or is connected to something else. Here it's connected to the word 'ears' (آذَانَ), meaning 'ears of livestock'
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding alif and changing vowels to damma
Added نَّ at the end to make it mean 'I will DEFINITELY command' (makes it stronger and future)
Added هُمْ to show who is being commanded
Added لَ for extra emphasis

steps.m: and I will surely command them
steps.n: Added وَ to connect this phrase with the previous one
Added a shadda (double letter) on ي and vowels to make it mean 'he changed' in past tense
Added يُ at start to make it present tense 'he changes' instead of past tense 'he changed'. The doubled middle letter ـيّـ shows it's done with extra effort/intensity
Added فَلَ (fa-la) at the start to mean 'and so/then' - showing this happens as a result of something

steps.m: and so he will surely change
steps.n: Added نَّ at the end to add emphasis, like saying 'definitely' or 'surely'

steps.m: to create or shape something from nothing

steps.n: Changed the verb into a noun meaning 'creation'. This word is what's being changed in the phrase 'they will change Allah's creation'.
Added fatha at the end to show this word is being acted upon by the verb 'they will change'
Changed to the proper name Allah by adding ال and merging letters. This is a unique word in Arabic that always refers to the One True God.
Added kasra at the end to show possession - this word is connected to خَلْقَ (creation) in the phrase 'creation of Allah'
This is a question word that became a relative pronoun meaning 'whoever' or 'who'. It connects the first part of the sentence to what comes after.
Added و with fatha at the beginning to mean 'and'. This word connects to يَتَّخِذِ (takes/adopts) in the next part of the verse.
Added vowels to make the basic past tense verb
The doubled ت shows this is done with more effort or intensity than just 'taking'
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels
Changed ending vowel to kasra to show jussive mood

steps.m: to rebel, be distant from good

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha to create the basic past tense verb

steps.m: one who constantly rebels/is distant from good
steps.n: Added ي after first letter and ا after second letter to create an intensive noun pattern showing someone who does this action excessively
Added ال to make it specific, referring to Satan himself. The ل merges into ش creating a doubled sound (shown by ّ)

steps.m: Satan (as the object being taken as an ally)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show this is the object of يَتَّخِذِ (takes/adopts)
Added vowels to connect letters and make it a noun for someone who protects
Adding shadda makes it stronger - someone who really protects/guards closely
The tanwin shows this is indefinite (any protector). This word is the object (accusative case) of the verb يتخذ (takes/adopts).
This is a preposition that shows something comes from somewhere else
Added shadda for emphasis and to help connect with the next word. In this ayah, it's used with دُونِ to mean 'instead of' or 'other than' Allah.

steps.m: to be lower or beneath something
steps.n: This root gives us words about being lower, beneath, or less than something else
Adding the damma (ُ) vowel mark changes this into a word meaning 'beneath' or 'lower than'
Added kasra (ِ) at the end to show this connects to the next word (اللَّهِ). Together they mean 'besides/other than Allah'
Changed to the proper name Allah by adding ال and merging letters. This is a unique word that became a proper name for God.

Added kasra at the end to show possession - this word is connected to خَلْقَ (creation) in the ayah, showing that creation belongs to Allah. Also added shadda on ل and changed initial alif to wasl form.
This is an emphasis particle that shows something definitely happened or is certain. It's like adding 'indeed' or 'certainly' in English.

steps.m: then certainly (connects to خَسِرَ - has lost)
steps.n: Added فَ (fa) at the beginning to show this is a result of what came before. In this ayah, it connects to the verb خَسِرَ (has lost) to show the consequence.
Added vowels to connect the letters and show this is a past tense verb. This word comes after فَقَدْ (then certainly) in the ayah to show a definite result.
This is already in its basic past tense form with the correct vowels

steps.m: to lose or be at a loss

steps.n: Changed the word into a noun showing a complete state of loss

steps.m: a complete loss (object of فَقَدْ خَسِرَ - has certainly suffered)
steps.n: Added tanween (double vowel mark) to show this is the object - it tells us what kind of loss was suffered in فَقَدْ خَسِرَ (has certainly suffered)
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Added مُ prefix and changed vowels to show this describes someone/something that does the action of making clear
Added shadda on م because it comes after a word ending in a vowel
Added tanween alif to show this word is describing how the loss happened in خُسْرَانًا (loss)

steps.m: to promise or make a commitment

steps.n: Changes from past to present tense by adding ي
Added هُمْ at the end to show who receives the promise - in this case referring to those who Satan misleads
Adding shadda intensifies the meaning and makes it about causing desires in others
Changed to present tense. The يُ shows it's happening now or in the future. This word connects to يَعِدُهُمْ (he promises them) in the verse.

steps.m: and he arouses desires (subject of يَعِدُهُمْ)
steps.n: Adds conjunction 'and'

steps.m: and he arouses desires in them (subject of يَعِدُهُمْ)
steps.n: Adds هِمْ meaning 'them' to show who is affected by the action. This connects to the same 'them' in يَعِدُهُمْ (he promises them).
Added fatha to show this is the basic negative particle ما that makes something 'not' happen. Here it negates the verb يَعِدُ (promises), showing the devil's promises are not real.
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase to the previous one (يَعِدُهُمْ - he promises them). It shows these actions happen together.

steps.m: to promise or make a pledge

steps.n: Added vowels to connect the letters and show past tense
Changed to present tense. The و drops because it's a weak letter at the start of a present tense verb. This verb connects to يُمَنِّيهِمْ (he gives false hopes to them) in the ayah.
Added هُمُ at the end to show who receives the promise. The extra ُ helps connect smoothly to the next word الشَّيْطٰنُ (Satan).
Added ي after first letter and ا after second letter to create a noun meaning someone who embodies this quality extremely

steps.m: one who is extremely rebellious (as the doer)
steps.n: Added ُ to show this is the subject doing the action in يَعِدُهُمْ (he promises them)

steps.m: the devil (as the doer of يَعِدُهُمْ)
steps.n: Added ٱل with shadda to make it specific and refer to Satan himself

steps.m: the devil (as the doer of يَعِدُهُمْ)
steps.n: Changed alif to alif maqsurah for writing style
Added shadda to double the first consonant after the definite article
This is a basic word used to show exceptions - like saying 'but not' or 'except for' in English
Adding ا at the end makes the word connect better with what comes after it. Here it connects with غُرُورًا (deception) to mean 'except deception'
Changed into a noun that means the result of tricking someone. This word is what the Shaytan (devil) promises, as mentioned in يَعِدُهُمُ (he promises them).

steps.m: false promises (as the object of يَعِدُهُمُ)
steps.n: Added ًا at the end to show this word is receiving the action of promising. The Shaytan (devil) promises them these false things.
This is the basic form of the word that means 'those'
The alif with line (ٰ) shows that these things are far away
The small alif (ٓ) helps with pronunciation
The ئِكَ ending makes this word point specifically to a group being talked about. In this ayah, it points to the people whose destination (مَأْوَىٰهُمْ) is being described.
Added مَ at the beginning to show this is a place where the action happens, like how 'study' becomes 'school' (a place to study)
Added a small alif (ٰ) above the ى to help with pronunciation

steps.m: their place of shelter (referring to those mentioned in أُو۟لٰٓئِكَ)
steps.n: Added هُمْ at the end to show this belongs to them (the people mentioned before)
Added vowel marks to show pronunciation. This is a proper name for Hell that doesn't come from an Arabic root - it's believed to come from Hebrew 'Gehenna'.
Added ُ (damma) at the end to show this is the subject of the sentence. In this ayah, Hell is being described as their abode (مَأْوَىٰهُمْ means 'their refuge/dwelling place').
This is the basic negation word لا that means 'not'
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this negation to the previous statement. This word negates the verb يَجِدُونَ (they find) that comes after it.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense form
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels

steps.m: they find (referring to the disbelievers in جَهَنَّمُ)
steps.n: Added plural ending to show multiple people doing the action. This refers to the disbelievers mentioned earlier who are in Hell.
This is a basic preposition that shows something moving or being distant from something else
Added ها at the end to show what we're moving away from. Here it refers back to جَهَنَّمُ (Hell) mentioned earlier in the verse.
Adding مَ at the start and changing the vowels shows this is a place or way of doing the action

steps.m: a place/way of escape (object of يَجِدُونَ)
steps.n: Adding ًا shows this word is being acted upon by the verb يَجِدُونَ (they find)
This is the basic form of the word that means 'who' or 'which'. It's used to describe or point to someone or something.
Added ال at the beginning and doubled the ذ to make it specific. This changes it from just 'who' to 'the one who'.
Added ين at the end to make it plural, changing it from 'the one who' to 'those who'.
Added و at the beginning to connect this with what came before. This word is connected to ءَامَنُوا۟ (they believed) which comes right after it.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make it a past tense verb
Adding the alif at the start changes the meaning from 'trust' to 'believe/have faith', making it stronger

steps.m: they believed (connected to وَٱلَّذِينَ - those who)
steps.n: Added وا to show multiple people did the action. This word describes 'those who' (وَٱلَّذِينَ) from earlier in the verse.
Connected the root letters with vowels to make the basic past tense verb

steps.m: they did good deeds (referring to those who believed in previous ayah)
steps.n: The suffix وا changes 'he did' to 'they did', referring back to وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ (those who believed)

steps.m: and they did good deeds (connected to ءَامَنُوا۟)
steps.n: Adds و to connect this action with ءَامَنُوا۟ (believed) in the previous word, showing these people both believed AND did good deeds
This is the basic meaning of the root
Connected the letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Changed the word pattern to show someone who has this quality
Added the feminine plural ending to talk about multiple good deeds (this word is what is being acted upon by عَمِلُوا۟ - they did)
Added ال to point to specific righteous deeds. Also added shadda on ص because it comes after ال

steps.m: the good/righteous deeds (object of عَمِلُوا۟ - they did)
steps.n: Changed the ending to show this word is being acted upon by the verb عَمِلُوا۟ (they did)
This is the basic meaning of the root
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Added alif at beginning to show causing someone else to do the action
Changed to present tense with 'we' as the subject
Added future marker to show this will happen later
Added pronoun suffix to show who is being made to enter

steps.m: to cover or hide something completely
steps.n: This is the basic meaning of the root
Added vowels (fatha) and doubled the middle letter (ن) to make a past-tense verb meaning 'it covered'
Added ة to make it a noun for a place that is covered/hidden, which came to mean 'garden' because gardens are enclosed spaces

steps.m: gardens (object of سَنُدْخِلُ - we will admit)
steps.n: Changed to plural form to show multiple gardens. This is what will be entered into (object of سَنُدْخِلُ)
Added the tanwin kasra to show this is the object being acted upon (what is being entered into)
This is the basic meaning of the root
Changes from base root to a present tense verb meaning 'she/it flows'
This is a helper word that shows where something starts or comes from. In this ayah, it connects with تَحْتِهَا (beneath it) to show the gardens have rivers flowing from beneath them.
Basic concept of being underneath something
Adding vowel marks to make the word readable - fatha on تَ and sukun on حْ
Added هَا to show what is underneath. This word appears in the phrase 'gardens beneath which rivers flow', where it tells us the rivers are under the gardens.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Changed from a verb (flowed) to a noun (river) by changing the middle vowel
Changed to plural form by adding أ at start and ا after second letter
Added ال to make it specific and ُ to show it's connected to the gardens mentioned. The ا is written as ٰ in this style.
This is the basic meaning of the root
Changed root into active participle (doer) form by adding alif after first letter
Makes the word refer to multiple people (masculine plural)
Adds fatha mark to make the word grammatically correct in this sentence

steps.m: those who remain forever (as the object of سَنُدْخِلُهُمْ/We will admit them)
steps.n: The alif changed to a long alif (ٰ), but the meaning stays the same - it still means 'those who remain forever' and describes who is being admitted
This is a helper word that shows something is inside or within something else (in this case, showing that they will be inside gardens/جَنَّاتٍ)
Added the kasra vowel mark under ف to show how it's pronounced
Added هَآ which means 'it' (feminine) to show what something is in. Here it refers back to the gardens (جَنَّاتٍ) mentioned earlier.
Connected the letters with fatha marks to form the basic noun

steps.m: forever (describing how long they will stay in جَنَّاتٍ)
steps.n: Added ًا at the end to make it an adverb meaning 'forever' - it tells us how long they will stay in the gardens
This is the basic meaning of the root
Added vowel marks to transform the root into a noun meaning 'promise'. This word is described as حَقًّا (true/real) in this ayah.
Added fatha at the end because this word is the object being acted upon in the sentence
Added ال to show this is THE one true deity, and the hamza was dropped because this is a special divine name. The middle letter gets a shadda to show emphasis.
Added kasra at the end because it comes after the word مِنَ (from/than) in this ayah. The alif at the start changed to a special connecting alif (ٱ).
This is the basic meaning of the root
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb
Changed from a verb (he was true) to a noun (truth) by dropping a letter and adding shadda
Adding tanween alif changes this into an adverb describing how true Allah's promise is
This is a relative pronoun that introduces a question or condition about people. Here it connects to أَصْدَقُ (more truthful) in the next part of the ayah.

steps.m: and who/whoever (connects to أَصْدَقُ - more truthful)
steps.n: Added 'and' at the beginning to connect this phrase to the previous statement. The full meaning flows as 'And who is more truthful...'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb

steps.m: more truthful (than مِنَ ٱللَّهِ - than Allah)
steps.n: Added أَ at beginning and changed vowels to make it comparative. In this ayah, it's comparing truthfulness with Allah, showing that no one is more truthful than Allah.
Basic form of the word that shows where something starts or comes from
Added َ (fatha) at the end to help connect it smoothly to the next word (اللَّهِ). This combination means 'from Allah'
Added shadda to show emphasis on the L sound, which is special for this divine name
Added kasra at the end to show possession - this word owns or possesses the promise (وَعْدَ) mentioned in the ayah
This is the basic meaning of the root
Changed from basic root meaning 'speak' to noun meaning 'a saying' by adding vowels
Changed the vowels to show this is happening to something rather than someone doing it

steps.m: in speech (describing وَعْدَ اللَّهِ - Allah's promise)
steps.n: Changed from 'being said' to 'in speech' by adding tanween (two dots) at the end - shows it describes how something is done
Adding vowels to make it a usable past tense verb meaning 'is not'
Changes the word to show many wishes
The doubled letter ّ makes the meaning stronger
كُمْ shows the wishes belong to multiple people being spoken to

steps.m: by your strong wishes (negated by لَّيْسَ)
steps.n: بِ shows how something happens. Here it's negated by لَّيْسَ (it is not)
This is a negative particle that makes things negative
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this phrase to what came before it in the ayah
The alif is lengthened for emphasis when stopping at this word
Forms basic noun pattern for a single instance of wishing
Changes from single wish to multiple wishes by adding 'a' sound at start and middle of word (broken plural)
Adds emphasis by doubling the final letter
Shows this word follows بِ (with/by) in the ayah
Added fatha on first letter and sukun on second letter to make it a noun meaning 'people/family'
Added kasra at the end to show this word is possessed by الكتاب (the Book), forming the phrase 'People of the Book'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Changed the pattern to make a noun for the result of writing
Added the definite article ال to specify this particular book
Added kasra at the end to show this book belongs to or is connected to something (in this case, أَهْل meaning 'people')
This is a special word that introduces a condition about people. In this ayah, it connects to the verb يَعْمَلْ (does/works) to mean 'whoever does'.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the past tense verb
Changed to present tense with يَ at start. The sukun at the end shows this is in a conditional sentence, connected to 'whoever' (مَن) in the ayah
Changed the action into a noun meaning the evil deed itself. This word is what's being done in يَعْمَلْ (does/commits)
Added marks to show this is 'an evil deed' that receives the action of the verb

steps.m: to give back what is due (reward or punishment)
he gave back what was due
steps.n: Added vowel marks to connect the root letters into a basic past tense verb

steps.m: he is given back what is due
steps.n: Changed to passive voice by adding يُ at start and changing vowels. This shows the person is receiving the action rather than doing it.

steps.m: he is given back what is due (in response to سُوٓءًا/evil)
steps.n: The final ى is dropped because this word is in a special grammar position (after مَن). This doesn't change the core meaning.
This is a preposition that shows how something is done
Added هِ to show what the preposition applies to

steps.m: with it/by it (object of يُجْزَ - will be recompensed)
steps.n: Added a small mark (ۦ) to help with pronunciation. This word shows what someone will be recompensed with in the ayah.
This is a negative particle that makes the verb يَجِدْ (find) negative, changing it from 'find' to 'will not find'
Added وَ (and) at the start to connect this negative statement with what came before it in the verse
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make it a past tense verb
Added يَ at the start and changed vowels to show present tense. The و drops because it's a weak letter at the start.
Changed the final ُ to ْ because this word comes in a conditional phrase (after مَن meaning 'whoever')
This is a basic particle that shows direction or purpose
Added هُ to show who the action is for

steps.m: for him/to him (referring to finding a protector in this ayah)
steps.n: Added a small mark (ۥ) that helps with pronunciation but doesn't change the meaning. This word relates to يَجِدْ (find) in the ayah, showing who won't find a protector.
This is a preposition that shows where something comes from or a relationship between things. In this ayah, it connects with the word دُونِ to mean 'besides' or 'other than'.
Added damma to show this is a preposition meaning 'besides' or 'other than'
Added kasra at the end because this word is connected to اللَّهِ (Allah) that comes after it, showing it means 'besides Allah'
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'a deity'
Added ال to make it definite - 'the' god instead of 'a' god
The word Allah (ٱللَّه) comes from the word for 'god' (ٱلْإِلَه), with the kasra ending showing it belongs to something else

steps.m: to be close to and protect

steps.n: Adding vowels makes it a past tense verb showing someone protected or was close to something
Adding shadda makes it a noun meaning someone who intensely protects/guards

steps.m: a protector/guardian (as object of يَجِدْ - he finds)
steps.n: Added tanween fatHa to show this word is being acted upon by the verb يَجِدْ (he finds) in the phrase 'he will not find a protector'
This is a negative particle that negates the verb يَجِدْ (he finds) that comes after it. It's one of the basic building blocks of Arabic that can't be broken down further.
The و at the beginning means 'and', connecting this negative word to the previous statement. Together with يَجِدْ it means 'and he will not find'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Added ي between second and third root letters and changed middle vowel to kasra to show someone who helps a lot
Added tanween alif to show this is the object being acted upon in the phrase 'will not find a helper'
This is a word that refers to any person. In this ayah, it's the one who will do the righteous deeds (يَعْمَلْ)
Added و at the beginning to connect this part to what came before it, like saying 'and' in English
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb

The يَ at the start shows present/future tense. The sukun marks (ْ) show this is in a special grammar position after 'whoever' (وَمَن). This word is part of a conditional statement - 'whoever does...'
This is a preposition that shows where something comes from or what it belongs to. Here it connects to 'the righteous deeds' (ٱلصَّالِحٰتِ)
Added َ (fatha) at the end because the next word starts with ال. This helps connect the words smoothly when reading.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make a past tense verb
Changed pattern to show someone who has this quality
Made plural feminine form to refer to multiple good deeds or actions
Added 'the' to specify these particular good deeds
Added kasra at the end because it comes after مِنَ (from/of)
This is a preposition that shows where something comes from or what it belongs to. In this ayah, it connects to ٱلصَّالِحٰتِ (righteous deeds) to show which group or category is being talked about.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the noun form meaning 'male'
Added tanwin (double kasra) at the end to show this is indefinite (like saying 'a male' instead of 'the male') and is the object of the word مِن (from/of) in this ayah
This word gives a choice between two things: male (ذَكَرٍ) or female (أُنثَىٰ). It works like 'or' in English when listing options.
Changes from root meaning into noun for 'female'
Added alif khanjariyah (small alif) above the ى which is just a writing variation that doesn't change meaning
This is the basic pronoun meaning 'he'. In this ayah, it's the subject of مُؤْمِنٌ (believer).
The و connects this phrase to what came before it, showing this is an additional condition being described
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a basic past tense verb
Added an alif at the start to change from 'feeling safe' to 'actively believing in something/someone'
Changed the word to show who does the action instead of the action itself
Added double-damma to show this is indefinite (like saying 'a believer' instead of 'the believer'). This word is the subject of وَهُوَ (and he/it is) in this ayah.
This is the base form meaning 'first' or 'primary' which evolved into a pointing word
These are actually two different words - not a transformation. أُولَى means 'first' while أُولٰٓئِكَ is a separate word meaning 'those' used to point to things far away
The فَ shows this is a result of the good deeds mentioned before. These people will enter Paradise as a result.
Connected the root letters with fatha to create past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels
Added ون to show multiple people are doing the action. This word describes what 'those people' (فَأُو۟لٰٓئِكَ) will do.
Added vowels and doubled the middle letter to show strong concealment
The ة changes this from an action to a place that has that quality - a garden is a protected/concealed space
The ال makes it refer to a specific garden - in this case, Paradise itself
The َ at the end shows this is the object being entered in the phrase 'they will enter Paradise'
This word makes something negative
The و connects this to the previous statement. This word negates يُظْلَمُونَ (they are wronged) to mean 'and they will not be wronged'

steps.m: to treat unfairly or harm someone's rights

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Changed to passive present tense by adding يُ at start and changing vowels
Added ون to show multiple people are receiving the action

steps.m: to make a hole by pecking or digging

steps.n: Added vowels to connect the letters and make it a past tense verb
Changes from verb 'to peck' into noun meaning 'mark from pecking'

steps.m: a tiny mark/amount (that's being acted upon)
steps.n: Added ًا to show this is the object being acted upon in the sentence. In this ayah, it shows that they won't be wronged even by the tiniest amount (like the mark a bird makes when pecking)
This is a question/conditional word that asks about or refers to people. In questions it means 'who?' and in statements it means 'whoever'
Added و at the beginning to connect this to what came before. This word is asking 'who' in relation to being better (أَحْسَنُ) in the following phrase.
Connected the root letters with vowels to make a basic past tense verb
Added أَ at the beginning and changed vowels to make comparative/superlative form. In this ayah, it describes دِينًا (religion/way of life) as being better/best.

steps.m: to follow a path or way

steps.n: The kasra and long vowel ي create a noun meaning a complete system or way of doing things

steps.m: a way of life/religion (as better than - أَحْسَنُ)
steps.n: The ًا ending shows this word is being used as a specification - it answers 'in what way is something better?'
Starting with two separate words: مِن (from/than) and مَن (who). In this ayah, it's comparing who could be better in religion.
When مِن comes before a word starting with م, the ن changes into another م with a shadda to make it easier to say
Added a shadda on the first م and a sukun at the end to show where to stop when reading
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb

steps.m: he submitted/surrendered (himself) (action related to وَجْهَهُۥ - his face)
steps.n: Adding أَ at the beginning changes the meaning to actively doing the action to oneself. In this ayah, it's specifically about submitting one's face (وَجْهَهُۥ) to Allah.
Added vowel marks to the root letters to make the word mean 'face' instead of 'to face'
Added fatha at the end to show this word is being acted upon by the verb أَسْلَمَ (submitted)
Added the possessive pronoun هُ with extension mark ۥ to show ownership. This word is what was submitted to Allah in the phrase 'submitted his face to Allah'
This is the base word Allah, which is a proper name and doesn't come from a root
Added لِ at the beginning to show direction or purpose
When لِ combines with الله, the ا is dropped and the ل gets a shadda. The final ِ shows this word is affected by something else in the sentence - here it's affected by the verb أَسْلَمَ (submitted).
This is a pronoun that means 'he'. In this ayah, it's the subject of مُحْسِنٌ (good-doer)
Added و at the beginning to connect this word to what came before it, meaning 'and'

steps.m: to do good, to be excellent

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Added مُ at the start to change from 'did good' to 'one who does good' - like changing 'help' to 'helper'

steps.m: one who does good (subject of وَهُوَ - and he is)
steps.n: Added double damma to show this is the subject of وَهُوَ (and he is) in this ayah
Added vowels to connect the root letters and show this happened in the past
Added ا at start and doubled the ب (with shadda) to show following with more intensity and devotion

steps.m: and he followed intensely/devotedly (مِلَّةَ - the way/religion)
steps.n: The و (and) was added at the start to connect this word to what came before it

steps.m: to follow a way or path

steps.n: Changes from general concept of following to specific religious path

steps.m: religious way or tradition (object of اتَّبَعَ - followed)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show this is the object of the verb 'followed' in the ayah
This is a proper name of Hebrew origin (אַבְרָהָם) that has been naturalized into Arabic. It does not follow Arabic root patterns.
