steps.m: and what (that was sent down to you)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it. In this ayah, it's connecting to the previous statement about what was revealed.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a basic past tense verb
Changed vowels to show this happened to something rather than someone doing it
Added hamza with damma at start to maintain the passive meaning. This word is what the believers (يُؤْمِنُونَ) believe in.
Basic preposition showing where something starts or comes from. In this ayah, it connects to what the believers (يُؤْمِنُونَ) believe from previous revelations.
Added fatha and sukun to make it a noun meaning 'before', with kasra showing it follows مِن (from)
Added ك to show this refers to 'you' (masculine singular). The whole phrase مِن قَبْلِكَ means 'from before you'
Added مُ at beginning to show who does the action, and ِ in middle to show it's happening now or regularly
Added ين to show we're talking about many people who do this

steps.m: those who establish/maintain (plural, in a grammatical state)
steps.n: Added the vowel mark to show this word's role in the sentence

steps.m: the ones who establish/maintain (object of يُؤْمِنُونَ)
steps.n: Adds 'the' (ال) to specify a particular group who establish/maintain

steps.m: and the ones who establish/maintain (object of يُؤْمِنُونَ)
steps.n: Added وَ (and) to connect this with the previous things that are believed in
Adding basic past tense vowel pattern
Added ة to make it into a noun for a single instance of prayer
Added ٱل ('the') at the start to make it specific/definite
Changed to proper spelling with long alif
Added a small diagonal line (fatha) at the end to show this is receiving the action of 'they believe in'
Connected the root letters together
Changes verb 'to give' into 'one who gives'
Makes the word plural - more than one giver

steps.m: and those who give (connected to ٱلرَّاسِخُونَ - those firmly grounded)
steps.n: Added و (and) and ال (the) to connect this word to the previous groups mentioned
Connected the root letters with fatha to create basic past tense verb
Changed the word to a noun form that means the act itself, with ة showing it's a specific religious practice
Added ٱل to make it specific and doubled the ز with shadda to show it's the well-known religious duty
Added fatha at the end to show this is the object of the verb يُؤْمِنُونَ (they believe in)

steps.m: to trust, feel safe, believe in

steps.n: Changes the word to mean someone who believes or trusts. The مُ at the start and the vowels show who does the action.
Adding ُونَ makes it refer to a group of believers. This word is the subject (doer) of the verb يُؤْمِنُونَ (they believe) in this ayah.
Adds 'the' (ال) to make it specific believers. The ٱ shape is used since it starts the word.
Adds coordinating conjunction meaning 'and'
This is the proper name of God in Arabic. The shadda shows emphasis on the ل.
The small diagonal line (ِ) under the last letter shows this means 'of Allah' or 'belonging to Allah'
The بِ at the beginning means 'in' or 'by', and ٱ is just a different way of writing ال. This word is what they believe in (يُؤْمِنُونَ meaning 'they believe').
Connected the letters with vowel marks to make the basic noun for 'day'
Added 'the' to make it specific
Added 'and' at the beginning to connect it with the previous phrase about believing in Allah

steps.m: and the day (connected to believing in Allah)

Adding ال makes it specific (like 'the' in English). The ِ at the end connects it to 'Day' in the phrase 'the Last Day'
This is the basic form of the demonstrative pronoun used to point to something far away
The alif with overbar (ٰ) extends the sound to emphasize distance
The ئِ adds specificity to show we're pointing to particular things or people

steps.m: those (referring to the believers mentioned before)
steps.n: The كَ at the end makes it more specific and connects it to what was mentioned before (in this case, the believers)

Added يُ at beginning to make it 'he gives' (present tense), with hamza (ؤ) to keep the 'a' sound
Changed يُ to نُ to show 'we' instead of 'he'
Added سَ to show future tense
Added هِمْ to show the object (them)

steps.m: to pay, reward, or compensate for work

steps.n: Changed the verb into a noun meaning the reward or payment itself

steps.m: a reward (object of سَنُؤْتِيهِمْ - we will give them)

Added vowels to connect the root letters and show this is a verb about becoming great

This is a special word that adds emphasis to what comes after it, like adding 'definitely' or 'surely' in English
Added آ at the end to show 'we' are doing the action. This connects to the next word أَوْحَيْنَآ (we revealed/inspired) where we are also the ones doing the action.

Adding hamza at beginning makes it causative (Form IV), changing from just 'reveal' to 'cause to reveal'

steps.m: just as, in the same way (connecting to أَوْحَيْنَآ)
steps.n: Adding ما makes the comparison more specific and emphatic. It connects the previous statement about revelation with what follows.

steps.m: just as, in the same way (connecting to أَوْحَيْنَآ)
steps.n: The alif is written as آ, which is just a spelling variation that doesn't change the meaning.
Connected the root letters with vowels to make a basic past tense verb
Added أ at the beginning to make it mean 'caused to reveal' instead of just 'revealed'. The ى is how we write ي at the end of words.

This is a basic preposition showing direction or destination. In this ayah, it connects to نُوحٍ (Noah) to show the revelation was sent to him.

This is a proper name that doesn't have a traditional Arabic root. The damma on ن is for pronunciation.
The double kasra (ٍ) shows this is the object of the word 'to' (إِلَىٰ) in 'We revealed to Noah'

Makes the word plural to show many prophets. This word is being acted on by أَوْحَيْنَآ (We revealed) in this ayah.
Adds ال to show we're talking about specific prophets, not just any prophets. The ن gets doubled (shadda) because it's a special letter that combines with ل

steps.m: and the prophets (object of أَوْحَيْنَآ - We revealed)
steps.n: Adds 'and' (و) at the start to connect it with what came before
Adds َ at the end to show this word is receiving the action of revelation (أَوْحَيْنَآ - We revealed)

steps.m: to be after or distant from something

Added kasra at the end to show this word is owned by or connected to something else
Added هِ at the end to show this means 'after him', referring back to Noah who was mentioned earlier

steps.m: to reveal or inspire divine guidance

Added a small alif (ٓ) to show the sound is stretched when connecting to the word نُوحٍ (Noah) that follows it

steps.m: Isaac (a prophet mentioned in the Quran)

steps.m: and the tribes (descended from Jacob's sons)

This is a proper noun borrowed from Aramaic
The و prefix means 'and', connecting this name to the previous ones

steps.m: and Job (object of أَوْحَيْنَآ - we revealed to)

steps.n: This is a proper name referring to Prophet Aaron (هارون), the brother of Prophet Moses (موسى). The long alif (ٰ) is a spelling variation of ا.

This is a proper noun borrowed from Hebrew

steps.n: Added و ('and') at the beginning to connect this to the previous prophets mentioned, and added َ at the end to show this is part of the object of أَوْحَيْنَآ (we revealed to)

steps.m: to give/bring (to cause someone to receive)

Adding sukun makes it a complete past tense verb
Adding نا at the end shows 'we' did the action
Adding وَ connects this to the previous statement about revelation (أَوْحَيْنَآ)

steps.m: messengers (as object of قَصَصْنٰهُمْ - we narrated about them)

steps.m: to tell stories or narrate events in sequence

Basic preposition meaning 'upon' or 'on'. This word doesn't come from a root - it's a basic preposition.
The ى changes to ي before adding attachments. This is just a spelling change that doesn't affect the meaning.

steps.m: upon you (object of نَقْصُصْ - we narrate)

Added the vowel mark kasra (ِ) under the م to show how to pronounce it correctly as 'min'
Changes from root to basic noun meaning 'before'

Added emphasis to the word by doubling the ل sound with a shadda
Added fatha vowels to connect the root letters into a past tense verb
Added 'ن' at the start to show 'we' are doing the action

steps.m: we narrate them (referring to the messengers mentioned before)

steps.n: This is a proper noun of non-Arabic origin (borrowed from Hebrew/Ancient Egyptian). It doesn't follow typical Arabic root patterns. The name possibly comes from Egyptian meaning 'born of' or 'child of', though some suggest it means 'drawn out of water' in Hebrew.

steps.m: Moses (the prophet mentioned in the Torah/Quran)
steps.n: Added diacritical marks to show correct pronunciation: damma (ُ) on م and fatha (َ) on س

steps.m: Moses (the prophet mentioned in the Torah/Quran)

Added tanween alif to show this describes how Allah spoke to Musa, emphasizing that it was direct speech
